proposals.title: Cross-Attention for Context: A Cross-Encoder-Inspired Bi-Encoder for Entity Linking
proposals.motivation: Bi-encoder architectures for entity linking, which encode mentions and entity descriptions independently, are efficient for retrieval but can lose critical contextual interaction. This is a known limitation in dense retrieval, as highlighted by work on the 'Lost in the Middle' problem and the role of retrieval in RAG systems. A model that fails to capture fine-grained dependencies between mention context and entity semantics may struggle with ambiguous or long-tail entities, degrading downstream task performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bi-encoder architecture incorporating a lightweight, asymmetric cross-attention mechanism from the mention context to the entity representation will capture richer semantic interactions than a standard bi-encoder, leading to higher retrieval accuracy without sacrificing inference-time efficiency.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid architecture where the entity encoder remains a standard transformer (e.g., BERT) processing Wikipedia entity pages independently. The mention encoder, however, will be augmented. It will first produce a contextual mention representation, then use a single cross-attention layer to attend to a fixed-size, pre-computed summary representation of the candidate entity (e.g., the [CLS] token or an averaged token representation). The resulting interacted representation is then used for the similarity score. We will train this model on standard entity linking datasets (e.g., AIDA-CoNLL) using a contrastive loss, comparing its performance against standard bi-encoders and full cross-encoders on both retrieval and end-to-end linking tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, efficient architecture that bridges the gap between bi-encoder efficiency and cross-encoder effectiveness for entity retrieval. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between interaction depth and speed, directly informing the design of retrieval subsystems for RAG. The findings will advance methods for building more accurate and scalable knowledge-intensive language models.
proposals.novelty: While cross-encoders are known to be more accurate, their inefficiency is a barrier. Our novelty lies in strategically relaxing the strict independence assumption of the bi-encoder *only* at a specific, lightweight point in the mention encoding process, a targeted hybrid approach not explored in mainstream entity linking models pre-2026.

proposals.title: End-to-End Entity Linking via Latent Knowledge Retrieval in Dense Language Models
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, as analyzed in IR-RAG @ SIGIR24, rely on a separate, often brittle, candidate retrieval step, which can fail or introduce latency. The 'Lost in the Middle' phenomenon further complicates the effective use of retrieved contexts. A single-stage model that directly links mentions to knowledge base entities would streamline systems and potentially improve accuracy by avoiding cascading retrieval errors.
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs), when fine-tuned with a specialized latent retrieval mechanism, can perform accurate entity linking directly from input text without an explicit, separate retrieval step, matching or exceeding the performance of multi-stage RAG pipelines.
proposals.method: We will adapt a dense encoder-decoder model (e.g., T5-XXL) by integrating a trainable, differentiable entity memory. The model will be trained on Wikipedia anchor-text datasets (e.g., KILT-EL) using a contrastive loss that pulls the representation of a mention close to its target entity's latent embedding while pushing it away from distractors. The process will involve discrete prompting techniques to structure the input (e.g., 'Link [mention] in context: ...') and evaluate generalization across model scales, as per Petroni's prior work on prompt generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architecture for end-to-end entity linking, demonstrating that accurate retrieval can be an emergent latent capability within a single model. It will offer a new benchmark for evaluating the inherent 'retrieval' performance of LLMs and deliver a simpler, more robust system design for knowledge-intensive language tasks.
proposals.novelty: While existing work uses LLMs as re-rankers or readers atop a retriever, this proposal posits that the retrieval step itself can be internalized and optimized jointly, challenging the assumed necessity of a separate candidate retrieval module in pre-2026 RAG systems.

proposals.title: Dual-View Dense Retrieval: Generating Hard Negatives via Entity Linking for Improved Passage Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Dense passage retrieval (DPR) is a cornerstone of modern RAG systems, but its performance is critically dependent on the quality of its training data, particularly hard negative examples. Manually curating effective hard negatives is expensive and often suboptimal, limiting model robustness. This work addresses this bottleneck by leveraging automated, scalable methods to generate high-quality, semantically challenging training data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an entity linking system, applied to a large corpus like Wikipedia, can automatically identify and generate challenging negative passages (e.g., those sharing entities but differing in core factual claims) that, when used to train a dual-encoder model, will yield more discriminative passage embeddings than those trained on standard or random negatives.
proposals.method: We will use a high-precision entity linking system (e.g., BLINK) to process Wikipedia passages. For a given query passage, we will identify its core entities and retrieve candidate negative passages that share at least one linked entity but are not relevant (based on distant supervision from hyperlinks). This creates a pool of entity-informed hard negatives. We will then train a standard bi-encoder (e.g., BERT-based) DPR model using a contrastive loss, comparing the standard in-batch negative training with our augmented strategy using these entity-hardened negatives. The retrieval performance will be evaluated on established benchmarks like Natural Questions and TriviaQA.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, automated pipeline for generating high-quality hard negatives using entity linking, reducing reliance on manual annotation or complex multi-stage training; 2) empirical evidence on the impact of entity-aware hard negatives on dense retrieval performance within the RAG pipeline, a core theme in Petroni's prior work on IR for RAG.
proposals.novelty: While hard negative mining is well-studied, using entity linking as a direct, unsupervised signal to *generate* hard negatives—rather than just select them from a static corpus—is a novel approach. It explicitly leverages structured knowledge to create a training signal that teaches models to distinguish between entity-related but factually distinct information, directly addressing the 'lost in the middle' problem for long contexts.

proposals.title: RIGER: Reinforcement-Grounded Iterative Retrieval-Verification for Hallucination-Free Generation
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), systems remain prone to hallucination, often due to uncritical reliance on retrieved passages. Existing methods typically use a single retrieval step or train retrievers on downstream QA loss, which can corrupt their core retrieval function and fail to enforce strict answer grounding. A principled, iterative architecture that explicitly separates and optimizes verification is needed to ensure verifiable outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative loop with a dedicated verifier module, which provides a hard attention mask for generation and a reward signal for reinforcement learning of the retriever, will significantly reduce hallucination while improving end-task performance compared to standard RAG and fine-tuning baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage system: 1) A dense passage retriever (e.g., DPR or Contriever) fetches candidates. 2) A verifier (a lightweight cross-encoder) scores each candidate's relevance, masking out low-scoring passages. 3) A generator (a frozen LLM) produces the answer conditioned only on the masked evidence. The retriever is then updated via REINFORCE, using the verifier's relevance scores as a reward, optimizing it to find passages that maximize verifier confidence without direct fine-tuning on generation loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, modular architecture for verifiable RAG that explicitly prevents the generator from attending to unverified text. It will also introduce a novel reinforcement learning objective for retrievers that aligns them with verifiability rather than end-answer likelihood, preserving their retrieval integrity.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the use of a separate verifier's scores as a reinforcement learning reward for the retriever, creating a feedback loop focused on evidence quality rather than end-answer correctness. This contrasts with existing joint training or pipeline methods that lack this explicit, iterative grounding mechanism.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Repairing the Internal Retrieval Mechanism of End-to-End RAG via Mechanistic Interpretability
proposals.motivation: End-to-end retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models promise seamless integration of knowledge retrieval and language generation. However, recent work, including 'Lost in the Middle,' shows that even models with long-context capabilities struggle to access information from the middle of their input. This failure mode undermines the reliability of integrated systems, creating a critical need to understand and fix the internal retrieval process, not just the external performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying mechanistic interpretability to an end-to-end RAG model will reveal specific, causal failure modes in its internal attention and information routing pathways, and that targeted architectural interventions based on these findings will measurably improve retrieval robustness, particularly for mid-context information.
proposals.method: We will first construct a controlled end-to-end RAG model, likely based on a FiD (Fusion-in-Decoder) or a late-interaction architecture, and train it on a multi-document QA task using Wikipedia. We will then apply a suite of mechanistic interpretability tools—including path patching, activation patching, and attention pattern analysis—to trace how retrieved passages are (or are not) utilized during generation. Based on identified bottlenecks (e.g., collapsed attention heads, faulty routing), we will iteratively co-design the model, testing interventions like constrained attention masks, auxiliary gradient signals to key retrieval layers, or modified aggregation mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, mechanistic account of retrieval failures in an integrated RAG system, moving beyond correlational metrics. It will also demonstrate a novel 'interpretability-guided design' paradigm for information systems, yielding a more robust model architecture and a public diagnostic framework for future RAG development.
proposals.novelty: While mechanistic interpretability has been applied to pure LMs and retrieval is studied in modular systems, this is the first proposal to dissect the *internal* retrieval act of an end-to-end RAG model. The closed-loop of using interpretability findings to directly engineer architectural fixes is a novel methodology for building reliable retrieval-integrated systems.

proposals.title: SparseGrams: Learning to Select Discriminative N-grams via Differentiable Gating for Efficient Document Representation
proposals.motivation: In retrieval and representation systems, n-gram features are foundational but lead to high-dimensional, noisy representations. Current methods, like bag-of-words or dense embeddings, either treat all n-grams equally or lack explicit, interpretable feature selection, which can harm efficiency and performance, especially in long-context scenarios where models struggle to utilize all information effectively. A principled method to learn a sparse, task-specific subset of n-grams is needed to improve discriminative power and computational efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural document encoder equipped with a differentiable gating mechanism, trained with a sparsity-inducing penalty, can learn to select a small, distinctive subset of n-grams that is more effective for document retrieval and representation than using the full n-gram set or standard weighting schemes.
proposals.method: We will implement a neural network that processes document n-grams through an embedding layer, followed by a gating layer (e.g., using a Gumbel-Softmax trick or L0 regularization) to produce a sparse mask over n-grams. The selected features are then aggregated into a document vector. The model will be trained end-to-end on a retrieval task (e.g., Wikipedia page retrieval or MS MARCO) using a contrastive loss, with an added L1 or L0 penalty on the gating weights to enforce sparsity. We will benchmark against TF-IDF, BM25, and dense retrievers, evaluating on recall, precision, and inference speed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable method for dynamic n-gram selection that improves retrieval efficiency and accuracy. It will yield insights into which linguistic features are most salient for document identity, bridging ideas from sparse neural encoding models in neuroscience to NLP. The resulting system will be a step towards more efficient and transparent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) backbones.
proposals.novelty: While sparse regularization is known, its application as a *learned, differentiable gating mechanism* for explicit n-gram selection in document representation is novel. This adapts a core technique from computational neuroscience—sparse feature identification in encoding models—to the distinct problem of selecting discrete linguistic units for retrieval, moving beyond static or heuristic feature weighting.

proposals.title: Surrogate Modeling for Retrieval: Distilling Dense Retriever Scoring into a Fast Autoregressive Generator
proposals.motivation: Deploying high-performance dense retrievers in latency-sensitive environments remains a challenge due to their computational cost. While model distillation exists, it often focuses on replicating final outputs, not the underlying scoring function for candidate text spans. This limits the ability to perform fast, on-the-fly generation and scoring of potential identifiers or n-grams, a core need for efficient retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and open-domain QA systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The scoring behavior of a large, accurate retriever can be captured by a small, fast autoregressive model trained as a surrogate, analogous to CFD surrogate models. This distilled model can emulate the retriever's conditional generative scoring process, enabling rapid n-gram generation and scoring with preserved quality.
proposals.method: We will treat a state-of-the-art dense retriever (e.g., Contriever, DPR) as the high-fidelity 'solver'. We will systematically probe it with a wide design space of queries and context passages from Wikipedia to generate a dense dataset of (query, context, candidate n-gram, relevance score) tuples. This dataset will train a compact Transformer-based autoregressive model (e.g., a distilled T5 variant) to predict the score distribution and generate high-scoring n-grams conditioned on the query and context, learning the retriever's implicit scoring function.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, fast surrogate model for retrieval scoring, enabling low-latency, high-quality candidate generation in RAG pipelines. It will provide a new paradigm for retriever distillation that focuses on function emulation rather than output mimicry, with empirical validation on tasks like entity linking and open-domain QA from pre-2026 benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting the rigorous surrogate modeling framework from computational science to information retrieval. Unlike standard distillation, it aims to train a small model to *generate* and score within the retriever's latent design space, a non-obvious shift from learning static rankings to learning a dynamic scoring process.

proposals.title: Distilling Dense Retriever Scoring into Autoregressive N-gram Generators for Efficient Lexical Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Dense retrievers like DPR and ColBERT achieve high accuracy but are computationally expensive for large-scale retrieval. Conversely, traditional lexical methods like BM25 are fast but lack semantic understanding. There is a critical need for retrieval models that are both performant and efficient, especially as RAG systems become ubiquitous. Knowledge distillation offers a promising path to bridge this gap by transferring ranking knowledge from a powerful teacher to a more efficient student model.
proposals.hypothesis: The scoring function of a large, slow dense retriever (teacher) can be effectively distilled into a smaller, faster autoregressive language model (student) trained to generate and score n-grams, enabling retrieval performance closer to the teacher with the latency profile of a lexical system.
proposals.method: We will use a pre-trained dense retriever (e.g., Contriever) as a fixed teacher to score (query, passage) pairs from a corpus like Wikipedia. The student will be a decoder-only T5 or a small GPT-2 model. It will be trained with a distillation loss (e.g., KL divergence) to match the teacher's relevance scores, conditioned on the query, to generate a weighted sequence of salient n-grams (e.g., unigrams and bigrams) for the passage. At inference, the student's generation probability for these n-grams will serve as a fast, learned lexical scoring function for retrieval.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel retrieval architecture that combines the efficiency of lexical matching with the semantic ranking quality of dense models. It will provide a concrete framework for distilling ranking knowledge into generative, identifier-based models. The resulting model will be evaluated on standard IR benchmarks (e.g., BEIR, MS MARCO) to demonstrate its trade-off between accuracy and speed.
proposals.novelty: While distillation for retrieval exists (e.g., TinyBERT), distilling a dense retriever's scoring into an autoregressive n-gram generator for direct lexical retrieval is novel. This approach moves beyond simply compressing the teacher's architecture and instead creates a new, highly efficient retrieval paradigm based on learned, generative term weighting.

proposals.title: LSH-Filtered Approximate N-gram Retrieval for Robust and Scalable Lexical Matching in RAG Systems
proposals.motivation: Robust retrieval over noisy text (e.g., OCR output, ASR transcripts, or adversarial misspellings) requires tolerant n-gram matching, but exhaustive edit-distance search over large vocabularies is computationally intractable for real-time systems. This bottleneck limits the performance and applicability of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems in noisy-data domains, a key concern highlighted in recent work on RAG architectures. An efficient, approximate solution is needed to enable scalable, fault-tolerant lexical retrieval.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage pipeline combining locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) for candidate filtering with bounded edit-distance verification can achieve near-exact retrieval accuracy for approximate n-gram matching while drastically reducing computational cost compared to a naive linear scan.
proposals.method: We will implement a system where input and vocabulary n-grams are first mapped to buckets using MinHash signatures for Jaccard similarity, parameterized to control recall. Candidate n-grams from matching buckets then undergo a second-stage verification using a bounded dynamic programming edit-distance calculation (e.g., Levenshtein with a threshold k=1,2). The system will be evaluated on noisy-text retrieval tasks using Wikipedia as a knowledge source, measuring precision, recall, and latency against a brute-force baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, tunable algorithm for efficient approximate string matching at scale, directly applicable to improving the robustness of the retrieval component in RAG pipelines. It will also offer an analysis of the precision-recall trade-offs governed by LSH parameters and edit-distance bounds, informing system design for real-time applications.
proposals.novelty: While LSH and edit-distance are known individually, their structured combination into a dedicated two-stage filter-verify pipeline for n-gram retrieval is novel, moving beyond simple embedding-based retrieval to address exact lexical variations efficiently, which is crucial for tasks where surface form matters.

proposals.title: Robust Information Retrieval in Language Models via Dual-Perturbation Training
proposals.motivation: Real-world information retrieval tasks, such as those in RAG systems, involve noisy, lexically varied text where key information is not optimally positioned. Pre-2026 research, including 'Lost in the Middle,' shows language models have strong positional biases, performing poorly when relevant information is at sequence extremes. This limits their robustness in practical applications where data is imperfect.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a model on sequences with both lexical fuzziness (e.g., typos, synonyms) and systematic positional inversion of key information will force the development of robust, content-sensitive search mechanisms, reducing reliance on static positional priors.
proposals.method: We will construct a training dataset from Wikipedia passages, applying two perturbations: (1) Fuzzy edits via character-level noise models and synonym substitution, and (2) 'Positional inversion' by moving the answer-bearing segment away from the middle to the beginning or end of the context. We will fine-tune a pre-2026 decoder-only LM (e.g., Llama 2) on a question-answering task using this data, analyzing changes in attention patterns and performance on held-out clean and perturbed evaluation sets from benchmarks like Natural Questions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training methodology for building more robust sequence models for IR and RAG. It will provide empirical evidence on how to mitigate positional bias and improve tolerance to lexical variation, leading to models better suited for noisy, real-world data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies positional bias or robustness to noise in isolation, our proposal's novelty lies in the *concurrent* application of lexical and positional perturbations during training, explicitly forcing the model to develop a fused, content-driven retrieval strategy.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Hierarchical Indices and Model Parallelism for Communication-Efficient Trillion-Token RAG
proposals.motivation: Scaling LLMs to trillion-token corpora for training and inference is fundamentally bottlenecked by communication overhead in distributed systems, as highlighted by the 'Lost in the Middle' problem and the critical role of retrieval in RAG. Current model and data parallelism strategies incur prohibitive latency and bandwidth costs when dynamically accessing massive, non-uniform corpora, limiting practical system scale. A fundamental redesign prioritizing communication-aware data placement is required.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a hierarchical, content-aware index structure with model parallelism, we can collocate frequently accessed data subsets with their corresponding model parameters in high-bandwidth pods, making the communication cost for retrieval-augmented workflows sub-linear in corpus size.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical, learnable index (e.g., based on IVF or HNSW) that partitions the corpus and maps partitions to specific model-parallel pods. We will develop novel asynchronous synchronization protocols and compressed communication primitives (e.g., leveraging top-k sparsification or error-compensated quantization) for gradient and activation exchange tailored to this hierarchical, retrieval-triggered workflow. The system will be evaluated on trillion-token synthetic corpora and large-scale Wikipedia-based tasks, measuring throughput, latency, and model performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel systems architecture blueprint for trillion-scale model training and inference, directly reducing the communication bottleneck. It will deliver new algorithms for asynchronous, compressed synchronization in retrieval-augmented workflows and a rigorous empirical analysis of the trade-offs between index locality, model accuracy, and system performance at unprecedented scale.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight co-design of the retrieval index topology with the model parallelism strategy, treating communication as a first-class constraint from the data structure upwards, rather than optimizing them separately. This moves beyond treating the corpus as a flat, uniformly accessible resource.

proposals.title: InverseIR: Learning to Generate Plausible Queries from Documents via Inverse Problem Formulation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval systems are trained to map queries to documents, but the inverse mapping—from documents to the queries that would retrieve them—is poorly understood. Explicitly modeling this inverse function is crucial for tasks like dataset augmentation for dense retrievers, analyzing document coverage for user intents, and improving the interpretability of retrieval models. This need is amplified in the RAG era, where understanding the latent query space a document can answer is key to performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural model can be trained to learn the conditional distribution P(query|document) from query-document pairs, and this learned inverse function will generate queries that are both diverse and semantically faithful to the source document, outperforming naive or heuristic generation methods.
proposals.method: We will frame query generation as an inverse problem, training a sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only LLM like LLaMA-2) on large-scale query-document pairs from information retrieval datasets (e.g., MS MARCO, Natural Questions). The model will be conditioned on the document text to generate multiple candidate queries. We will employ constrained decoding or a controlled text generation technique (e.g., PPLM) to ensure query-like brevity and interrogative form. Performance will be evaluated by using the generated queries to retrieve the original document against a BM25 baseline and a contemporary dense retriever (e.g., Contriever), measuring recall and precision.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, model-based approach for query generation, moving beyond simple term extraction or templating. It will yield a publicly available model and benchmark for the inverse retrieval task, and offer new insights into the relationship between document content and user intent that can inform the design of more robust retrieval and RAG systems.
proposals.novelty: While query generation has been explored, formulating it explicitly as an inverse problem from geophysics and training a dedicated neural model to approximate the inverse of the retrieval function is a novel conceptual and technical approach. It directly inverts the core objective of standard IR, which is typically only modeled implicitly.

proposals.title: DANIR: Dynamic Ambiguous N-gram Indexing for Real-Time Retrieval on Streaming Corpora
proposals.motivation: Real-time information retrieval on continuously updated corpora, such as news feeds or social media streams, is critical for modern applications like RAG systems. Traditional inverted indexes incur high update costs for each new document, making them inefficient for high-throughput streams. Furthermore, static n-gram models fail to adapt as the statistical context and meaning of shared phrases (e.g., 'Apple stock' vs. 'apple pie') evolve dynamically within the live corpus.
proposals.hypothesis: A retrieval index that maps ambiguous, shared n-grams to multiple documents, coupled with a real-time scoring model that leverages evolving corpus statistics for disambiguation, can achieve both lower update complexity and competitive retrieval accuracy compared to traditional dynamic indexing on streaming data.
proposals.method: We will design a Dynamic Ambiguous N-gram Index (DANI) that stores n-grams with their posting lists but forgoes immediate unique disambiguation during indexing. For retrieval, we develop a lightweight, online scoring model inspired by streaming language models and incremental TF-IDF variants. This model will perform real-time disambiguation by analyzing recent co-occurrence patterns and temporal frequency shifts of n-grams within a sliding window of corpus updates, directly integrating this contextual signal into the ranking function.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel indexing and retrieval architecture specifically optimized for low-latency updates on streaming text. It will provide a formal framework and evaluation benchmark for assessing retrieval performance in dynamic, ambiguous contexts, advancing the design of efficient components for real-time RAG and information systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the intentional use of ambiguity as a mechanism to reduce update costs, paired with a disambiguation process driven by the live, statistical context of the stream itself, rather than relying on a static knowledge base or expensive model re-indexing.

proposals.title: SALIENCE: Joint Task Learning with Dynamic Contextual Salience Prediction for Robust Long-Context Language Models
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in long-context language models, their ability to effectively utilize extended inputs remains inconsistent, as shown by the 'lost-in-the-middle' phenomenon. Current models lack a mechanism to dynamically identify and prioritize the most task-relevant segments of a long context, leading to performance degradation when critical information is not in privileged positions like the beginning or end. This limits the robustness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems and other information-intensive applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a model with an auxiliary objective to predict the most salient contiguous context segment for a given task will lead to a more adaptive and content-driven attention mechanism, thereby improving task performance and robustness across diverse information distributions in long documents.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based language model (e.g., T5 or LLaMA) with a lightweight, parallel salience prediction network. This auxiliary network will process encoded representations to score all possible contiguous windows in the input context. The primary model's attention scores will be softly biased (via a learned gating mechanism) to favor the highest-scored salient region. The model will be trained end-to-end with a dual loss: the standard task loss (e.g., for QA) and a supervised loss for the salience predictor, using heuristically derived salience labels from attention patterns or retrieval relevance scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for improving long-context utilization in LMs, moving beyond static positional biases. It will provide a new diagnostic framework for understanding how models allocate attention in relation to task relevance. The resulting models are expected to show improved performance on tasks like long-document QA and summarization, especially when key information is adversarially placed.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that analyzes attention patterns post-hoc or uses fixed positional masking, our method jointly learns to predict salience as an integral part of task training, creating a dynamic, content-aware prioritization signal that directly modulates the attention mechanism.

proposals.title: Salient Phrase Inversion: Training Language Models for Position-Agnostic Information Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including 'Lost in the Middle,' demonstrates that language models (LMs) struggle to retrieve information from non-standard positions in long contexts, a critical failure for robust RAG systems. Current training paradigms, which rely on standard document ordering, may reinforce positional biases. This project addresses the core challenge of making LMs' internal information retrieval mechanisms robust to unpredictable information placement, a necessity for real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training LMs on sequences where variable-length, information-rich phrases are deliberately placed in non-standard positions (e.g., the middle) will force the development of content-addressable memory mechanisms, leading to improved robustness and generalization on tasks where key information appears in unpredictable locations.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel training dataset from Wikipedia, using information extraction prompts to identify salient, variable-length phrases (e.g., key facts, definitions). We will systematically invert these phrases to non-standard positions (middle, random) within their context windows. We will fine-tune a base LM (e.g., Llama 2) on this dataset using a masked language modeling objective specifically targeting the salient phrases, combined with a retrieval-augmented task (e.g., question answering) to evaluate memorization and access. Performance will be benchmarked against standard models on curated long-context QA tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training methodology to improve the positional robustness of LMs for information retrieval. It will yield new insights into how LMs can develop content-based memory access, directly informing the design of more reliable RAG systems. We will release the inversion training dataset and code as a benchmark for future research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied positional bias, our method actively *trains against it* using a natural, phrase-based unit of meaning, combining the inversion challenge with a focus on intrinsic semantic salience rather than fixed n-gram patterns.

proposals.title: NARRATIVECHAIN: A Procedurally Generated Benchmark for Dynamic Reasoning in Long-Context Language Models
proposals.motivation: While models now support long contexts, benchmarks like L-Eval and LongBench primarily test static retrieval of isolated facts, not the dynamic comprehension needed for real-world tasks like analyzing documents or following multi-turn dialogue. The 'lost-in-the-middle' phenomenon shows models struggle with information in the middle of long contexts, but its impact on complex, narrative-driven reasoning is poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to assess the functional utility of long-context models for complex analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current long-context language models will show significant performance degradation on tasks requiring dynamic narrative tracking and causal inference compared to simple fact retrieval, and that this degradation is not adequately captured by existing benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop a procedural generation framework to create long, structured narratives (e.g., multi-character mysteries, evolving geopolitical scenarios) with controlled variables like entity density and causal chain depth. Inspired by Petroni's work on discrete prompts and retrieval, we will design a multi-stage evaluation protocol. This protocol uses sequences of interdependent questions that require tracking shifting perspectives, resolving ambiguous references, and making inferences based on information distributed across the entire narrative, directly testing models like GPT-4-Turbo, Claude-3, and open-source long-context variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first benchmark specifically designed to evaluate dynamic reasoning and narrative coherence in long contexts, moving beyond fact retrieval. It will deliver a diagnostic toolkit to pinpoint specific failure modes (e.g., temporal confusion, entity drift) in long-context models. The findings will inform the development of more robust retrieval and reasoning systems for RAG architectures.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing long-context benchmarks focused on retrieval, NARRATIVECHAIN evaluates the continuous, integrative reasoning required to maintain a coherent mental model of a narrative. Its use of procedurally generated, interleaved plots with controlled causal structures provides a systematic and scalable testbed not found in static QA datasets.

proposals.title: HIRES: Hierarchical Indexing for Retrieval-Enhanced Sampling to Mitigate Mid-Context Degradation in Long-Input LMs
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) with long-context windows exhibit a well-documented 'lost-in-the-middle' problem, where retrieval performance for information placed in the middle of the context collapses. This severely limits the practical utility of RAG systems and long-document processing, as critical information cannot be reliably accessed regardless of model scale. An architectural solution that dynamically allocates computational resources based on content, rather than static position, is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage, hierarchical sampling architecture, inspired by genomic sequence alignment, can mitigate mid-context degradation by using a lightweight indexing network to predict informationally salient segments, thereby enabling a primary model to focus its deep processing capacity on these candidate regions.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture with a first-stage Indexing Network (a small, causal transformer or state-space model) that performs a fast, single pass over the full input to output a sparse salience map. This map guides a Hierarchical Sampler, which selects and routes compact, high-salience context chunks (e.g., from the middle regions) to a second-stage, primary LLM for deep attention. The entire system is trained end-to-end on a multi-task objective combining language modeling and a needle-in-a-haystack retrieval task, using datasets like Multi-Document QA and curated long-context benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, bio-inspired neural architecture for efficient long-context processing that directly addresses the mid-context failure mode. It will provide empirical evidence on the viability of dynamic, content-aware computation routing over fixed attention patterns, advancing the design of performant RAG and information access systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the adaptation of hierarchical indexing and spaced sampling from computational genomics to neural network design for language, moving beyond positional interventions like sliding windows or fixed sparse patterns to a learned, input-adaptive strategy for computational resource allocation.

proposals.title: Data-Centric Scaling Laws: A Distributed Framework for Measuring Positional Bias Emergence in Trillion-Token Language Models
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including 'Lost in the Middle,' demonstrates that language models exhibit significant positional biases (e.g., poor retrieval from context middles) when using long contexts, but the origins of these biases in the training data distribution are poorly understood. As the field scales to trillion-token training on massive, heterogeneous web corpora, we lack principled methods to analyze how the *structure* of this data—not just its volume—shapes fundamental model behaviors like information retrieval and attention. This gap limits our ability to engineer training data for desired capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic variation in the positional distribution and length of information in trillion-token training mixtures will predictably alter the emergent positional attention biases and long-context retrieval performance of the resulting models, enabling data-centric control over these behaviors.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed data curation and training framework to construct controlled trillion-token mixtures from sources like Books3, GitHub code, and filtered web text (C4, RefinedWeb). We will algorithmically vary sequence lengths, information placement (primacy, recency, uniform), and document structure across mixture 'strata'. Using a scalable architecture like a GPT-3/LLaMA-style decoder, we will train a series of mid-scale models (e.g., 1-7B parameters) on these distinct mixtures. Each model will be instrumented with targeted probes—inspired by discrete information extraction prompts—to measure retrieval accuracy and attention entropy across all context positions during and after training.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel, open-source framework for large-scale, controlled data-mixture experiments, 2) the first empirical study directly linking trillion-token data structure to emergent positional dynamics, and 3) concrete guidelines for designing training corpora to mitigate pathological biases like the 'lost-in-the-middle' effect, advancing data-centric AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes bias in fixed models, we propose *engineering the training data itself at scale* as the independent variable. This fuses data-centric scaling research with fine-grained behavioral analysis, moving beyond aggregate loss to a causal understanding of how internet-scale data properties shape retrieval.

proposals.title: Cognitively-Grounded Tokenization: Learning Variable-Length Phrases via Psychometric Salience
proposals.motivation: Current subword tokenizers (e.g., BPE, WordPiece) are data-driven but lack a cognitive or linguistic grounding, segmenting text into units that may not align with meaningful information chunks. This can impair model efficiency and interpretability, especially in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) where the granularity of retrieved text chunks is critical for performance. A tokenization strategy that adaptively identifies cognitively salient, variable-length phrases could improve both language model pre-training and downstream tasks like information extraction and long-context understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Text sequences that induce a U-shaped 'serial position curve'—with high predictive utility at their boundaries (primacy and recency)—correspond to cognitively salient, informationally coherent units, and a tokenizer trained to segment text to maximize this psychometric signature will produce more efficient and interpretable representations than fixed-length or frequency-based methods.
proposals.method: We will operationalize psychometric salience by training a lightweight neural boundary predictor (e.g., a convolutional network over character embeddings) to score candidate segment boundaries. The training signal will be derived from the performance of a frozen, pre-trained language model (e.g., LLaMA-2) on a cloze or next-token prediction task across windows of text; sequences where the LM's accuracy follows a U-shaped curve will be labeled as high-salience units. This predictor will then guide a dynamic programming algorithm (like a Viterbi-style decoder) to segment text into variable-length tokens that maximize aggregate salience, creating a new token vocabulary.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, cognitively-inspired tokenization algorithm that produces adaptive, variable-length phrases. It will provide a new framework for evaluating tokenization quality beyond compression efficiency, linking it to psycholinguistic theory. The resulting tokenizer is expected to improve model performance on tasks sensitive to chunk granularity, such as retrieval and long-document understanding.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed LM internals (e.g., 'Lost in the Middle') or used frequency for tokenization, this is the first to directly apply the psychometric model of serial position curves to learn a segmentation strategy, creating a fundamental bridge between cognitive science and representation learning for NLP.

proposals.title: JOLI-DCM: Jointly-Optimized Latent Indexing for Dynamic Context Management in Long-Form Generation
proposals.motivation: Large language models struggle to utilize information from long contexts effectively, a phenomenon known as the 'lost-in-the-middle' problem. Existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems rely on static, pre-computed indexes that are not optimized for the specific task and model, creating a suboptimal retrieval bottleneck. This work addresses the need for a dynamic, task-aware mechanism to manage and access long-range dependencies during generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-optimizing a latent indexing mechanism end-to-end with a language model's primary task will yield a more performant and efficient system for long-context tasks than static retrieval methods, as the index will learn to capture precisely the contextual features most relevant for generation.
proposals.method: We propose a system where a small, trainable encoder network (e.g., a lightweight transformer) processes context chunks into latent vectors. These vectors are stored in a differentiable approximate nearest-neighbor index (e.g., based on FAISS). During autoregressive generation, the model's current hidden state queries this index to retrieve the k-most relevant prior context vectors, which are then fused into the generation process via cross-attention. The entire system—encoder, index parameters, and core LM—is trained jointly via gradient descent on a downstream objective (e.g., long-form QA, summarization).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end trainable architecture for dynamic context management, moving beyond static RAG. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of joint optimization for retrieval and generation, and release a framework for adaptive long-context modeling that could improve performance on tasks requiring long-range dependency resolution.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard RAG which uses a fixed, pre-trained retriever, our method's indexer is a neural component trained end-to-end with the generator. This departs from the established pipeline of separate retrieval and generation optimization, creating a fully integrated, task-adaptive context management system.

proposals.title: ALICE: Adaptive Latent Indexing for Contextual Bias Correction in Long-Context Language Models
proposals.motivation: Transformer-based language models exhibit a well-documented U-shaped attention bias, performing poorly on information located in the middle of long contexts, as shown in 'Lost in the Middle' (Liu et al., 2023). This fundamentally limits the reliability of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, a key research theme, where retrieved evidence can be placed anywhere within the context window. Current indexing methods for RAG are agnostic to this model-internal bias, leading to suboptimal information placement and degraded performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a latent indexing structure, whose design and query routing are explicitly conditioned on a target model's positional attention bias, can strategically place critical information to compensate for the model's weaknesses, thereby creating a synergistic system that achieves more uniform performance across the entire context window.
proposals.method: We will construct a two-tier learned index (inspired by learned index structures like PGM-index) over document chunks. The index's routing decisions will be trained using a loss function that combines standard retrieval metrics (e.g., recall) with a novel positional penalty term derived from the target LM's attention patterns (measured via gradient-based attribution on a diagnostic task). During inference, for a given query, the index will retrieve and order chunks, but also prepend adaptive 'pointer' tokens or summaries into the context at positions (e.g., very beginning) where the model's attention is most reliable, guiding it to content in 'difficult' regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel indexing framework that explicitly models and corrects for LM-internal biases, 2) a methodology for jointly optimizing retrieval systems and downstream LM performance beyond simple recall, and 3) empirical evidence on long-context tasks showing that adaptive information placement can mitigate the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon, advancing the design of performant RAG systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies the positional bias problem and others build indexes for retrieval, our approach is novel in creating a closed-loop system where the index's architecture is fundamentally shaped by and actively corrects for the specific attention limitations of the consumer language model, moving beyond retrieval-agnostic placement.

proposals.title: Structured Curriculum Learning via Outward-Inward Sequencing for Robust Information Extraction
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often struggle with information extraction from long, structured documents, showing performance degradation for facts located in the middle of contexts (Liu et al., 'Lost in the Middle'). This 'lost-in-the-middle' problem is critical for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, where precise extraction of central entities or relations from retrieved passages is paramount for factual accuracy. A systematic training curriculum to build robust structural understanding before tackling dense core information remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training language models using an outward-inward curriculum—first on data highlighting structural boundaries and peripheral information, then gradually on examples with complex, interdependent core information—will improve their robustness and generalization on structured information extraction tasks compared to standard training or monolithic curricula.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-stage curriculum for fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2, Mistral). Stage 1 uses synthetically constructed or Wikipedia-derived examples where the target information is exclusively at document edges, sentence boundaries, or graph leaves. Stage 2 introduces examples where targets are progressively closer to the center of textual or graph-based contexts. We will evaluate on established IE benchmarks (e.g., QA, relation extraction) and a new controlled dataset measuring 'middle-context' extraction accuracy, comparing against baselines of random ordering and simple-to-complex heuristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled curriculum learning strategy inspired by cross-disciplinary analogy, specifically tailored for structured NLP tasks. It will provide empirical evidence on whether explicitly teaching models about context boundaries improves their handling of central, dense information, with direct implications for the design of more reliable RAG and information retrieval systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific 'outward-inward' sequencing, a direct analogy from archaeological practice, which is distinct from existing curriculum learning based on difficulty metrics or token frequency. It explicitly decouples the learning of structural context from core semantic content, a non-obvious approach given prior work on prompt-based IE generalization and context window utilization.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Driven Bidirectional Synthesis for Systematic Retrieval-Augmented Training
proposals.motivation: Training effective retrievers and RAG systems requires high-quality, diverse (query, document) pairs, which are expensive to annotate. Recent work, including Petroni's analysis of RAG systems and the 'Lost in the Middle' phenomenon, highlights that model performance is highly sensitive to the structure and difficulty of information within documents. A systematic method to generate a curriculum of training data that mirrors educational progression is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: A curriculum learning framework that bidirectionally synthesizes queries and documents, starting from simple, salient document sections and progressing to complex central concepts, will autonomously create a training dataset that improves the sample efficiency and final performance of trained retrievers compared to training on randomly ordered or non-curated synthetic data.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline using a large language model (e.g., LLaMA-2, GPT-4) as a generator. Starting from a seed corpus (e.g., Wikipedia), we define a curriculum based on document position and conceptual complexity, informed by the 'Lost in the Middle' analysis. The model is first prompted to generate queries for the most salient sections (beginnings/ends), then for progressively more central and inter-dependent concepts. A reverse process generates pseudo-documents for canonical queries. We will use iterative prompting and self-evaluation loops to filter and rank the synthesized pairs, creating a structured dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, automated pipeline for generating structured, difficulty-ranked retrieval training data. It will yield a new methodology for curriculum-based training of retrievers and QA systems, and an empirical analysis of how training sequence based on information salience affects model performance on tasks like open-domain QA.
proposals.novelty: While query generation and data augmentation exist, the integration of a formal, position-and-complexity-aware curriculum with *bidirectional* synthesis for autonomous dataset creation is novel. It directly applies insights from language model failure patterns (e.g., lost in the middle) to design a training curriculum.

proposals.title: Beyond Textual Disambiguation: A Retrieval-Augmented Framework for Structured Output and Action Generation
proposals.motivation: Current RAG systems, as analyzed in work like IR-RAG @ SIGIR24, primarily retrieve passages to generate fluent, disambiguated text. However, many real-world tasks, such as database queries, API calls, or structured data extraction, require outputs in non-textual formats or executable actions. The assumption that all tasks require textual generation limits the integration of language models into automated workflows and downstream systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a retrieval-augmented generation system, explicitly trained or prompted to map retrieved information to structured templates or executable action sequences, will outperform standard text-generating RAG models on tasks requiring structured outputs, without sacrificing performance on traditional QA.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we adapt the retrieval process from Wikipedia or knowledge bases to fetch both relevant passages and, where available, associated structured data schemas (e.g., infoboxes). Second, we design and evaluate a suite of discrete, structured-output prompts—extending Petroni's work on prompt generalization—to guide language models (like GPT-4, Llama 2) to fill predefined JSON/XML templates or generate parameterized API call sequences. We will rigorously test this on a new benchmark combining existing long-context tasks (addressing 'lost in the middle' issues) with structured output requirements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for RAG that bridges the gap between unstructured text retrieval and structured system interaction. It will yield a public benchmark for evaluating structured-output RAG and demonstrate how prompt design can systematically shift model output modality, contributing to more reliable and integrable language model systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on RAG for text generation or discrete information extraction for QA, this proposal uniquely targets the systematic generation of executable actions and complex data structures as the primary output of a retrieval-augmented process, moving beyond disambiguation.

proposals.title: Parametric Knowledge or Implicit Indexing? Diagnosing the Need for Explicit Retrieval in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a dominant paradigm for grounding large language models (LLMs) in external knowledge, but it introduces latency, complexity, and potential for retrieval failures. Recent work, including 'Lost in the Middle', shows models struggle to utilize long, retrieved contexts effectively. This raises a fundamental question: for what tasks and knowledge types is explicit retrieval truly necessary versus a redundant overhead, given the extensive parametric knowledge and implicit reasoning capabilities of modern LLMs?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a significant subset of factual and procedural knowledge tasks, a sufficiently large and well-instructed language model can perform comparably to a RAG system by leveraging its parametric memory and internal reasoning, effectively acting as an implicit, compressed index, thereby challenging the default necessity of explicit retrieval.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled, multi-task benchmark spanning dense fact recall, multi-hop reasoning, and procedural knowledge from sources like Wikipedia. We will conduct a head-to-head performance, latency, and cost analysis between a state-of-the-art LLM (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 3) using carefully engineered prompts and the same model within a standard RAG pipeline. Crucially, we will employ diagnostic probes—inspired by discrete prompt generalization studies—to analyze model confidence and internal activation patterns when answering correctly without retrieval, distinguishing between true parametric recall and implicit reasoning chains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical framework for deciding when to deploy resource-intensive RAG systems versus optimized parametric models. It will contribute a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating implicit knowledge access and offer new insights into the trade-offs between model scale, instruction tuning, and architectural retrieval for building efficient knowledge systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes RAG components or long-context use, this proposal directly challenges the foundational assumption that explicit retrieval is always beneficial, framing the LLM itself as an alternative, implicit retrieval system. The novelty lies in the diagnostic, rather than comparative, approach to understanding *how* models succeed without retrieval.

proposals.title: Structured Knowledge Injection for Factual Consistency in KILT-RAG Pipelines
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems often rely on unstructured Wikipedia text, which can be verbose and contain contradictory or irrelevant information, leading to factual errors or inefficiency. The KILT benchmark provides a perfect testbed for integrating structured knowledge (e.g., infoboxes, Wikidata relations) to ground language models more precisely. This addresses a core challenge in RAG identified in prior work: improving the precision and trustworthiness of retrieved evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid retrieval strategy that jointly scores and retrieves passages from Wikipedia text and triples from its associated structured knowledge bases will lead to more factually consistent generations and higher performance on knowledge-intensive KILT tasks than retrieval from text alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the KILT framework to process Wikipedia infoboxes into a local knowledge graph of subject-predicate-object triples. For a given query, we will use a dual-encoder retriever (e.g., DPR) to retrieve text passages and a graph-augmented retriever (e.g., a transformer encoder over linearized triples) to retrieve relevant fact triples. A fusion module will combine these evidence streams, using the structured data to filter or re-rank the text passages before presenting a consolidated context to a generator like FiD or a T5 variant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available pipeline for hybrid structured/unstructured retrieval within KILT. It will deliver empirical evidence on the complementary roles of text and graphs for factual grounding, offering concrete design principles for more robust RAG systems. The findings will advance methods for mitigating hallucinations in knowledge-intensive language tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used knowledge graphs or text separately in RAG, systematically integrating both within the standardized KILT framework to explicitly study their interaction for factuality is novel. This moves beyond simple prompt-based extraction and instead modifies the core retrieval process.

proposals.title: REALM-RL: Reinforcement Learning for End-to-End Retriever Optimization in RAG Systems
proposals.motivation: Current Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, as highlighted in IR-RAG @ SIGIR24, often treat the retriever and reader as separate components, with the retriever trained on generic relevance signals (e.g., BM25, contrastive loss). This creates a misalignment between retrieval quality and final task performance, as documents useful for answer generation may not match simple keyword queries. Optimizing the retriever directly for downstream success is a critical open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a dense passage retriever with reinforcement learning, using rewards derived from the downstream reader's task performance (e.g., answer accuracy), will yield a retriever that retrieves context more useful for the final task, thereby improving overall RAG system performance.
proposals.method: We will frame retrieval as a sequential decision problem. A retriever agent (based on a bi-encoder like DPR) will propose a ranked list of documents given a query. The reader (a frozen LLM) will process the top-k documents to produce an answer. We will use REINFORCE or a PPO-based policy gradient algorithm, where the reward is a combination of task-specific metrics (e.g., exact match for QA) and a per-token reader likelihood penalty to encourage conciseness. The retriever's parameters will be updated to maximize the expected reward, using Wikipedia as a knowledge corpus for training and evaluation on benchmarks like Natural Questions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for end-to-end, task-aware retriever training without needing end-to-end differentiability. It will yield empirical evidence on whether RL-optimized retrievers mitigate issues like 'Lost in the Middle' by learning to prioritize critical evidence. The resulting system, REALM-RL, will be a new baseline for RAG performance.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been used for information retrieval, its application to optimize a neural retriever based on the black-box performance of a modern, non-differentiable LLM reader in a RAG setup is novel. This moves beyond supervised fine-tuning on static labels and directly addresses the retriever-reader objective gap.

proposals.title: GNN-EL: Enhancing Entity Linking via Graph Neural Networks over Wikipedia Link Structure
proposals.motivation: Entity linking (EL) is a critical component for robust RAG systems, as highlighted in your SIGIR24 work. Current methods often treat candidate entities in isolation, ignoring the rich relational structure inherent in knowledge sources like Wikipedia. This limits their ability to disambiguate entities in dense or ambiguous text, impacting downstream retrieval performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling Wikipedia's internal link structure as a graph and applying Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to propagate relational signals between candidate entities will significantly improve entity disambiguation accuracy compared to purely local, mention-context models.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed graph from Wikipedia, where nodes are articles (entities) and edges are hyperlinks. For a given document, we will extract mentions and generate a candidate subgraph of linked entities. We will then apply a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to this subgraph, encoding initial node features using pre-trained entity embeddings (e.g., from Wikipedia2Vec) and edge features from link semantics. The GNN will learn to aggregate neighborhood information to produce refined, context-aware entity representations for final linking decisions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, graph-aware entity linking framework that explicitly leverages relational knowledge. It will provide empirical evidence on standard benchmarks (e.g., AIDA-CoNLL) showing performance gains over flat retrieval methods, and offer insights into how structural information complements textual prompts for information extraction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used for knowledge graph completion, their application to the specific task of Wikipedia-based entity linking, integrated with modern prompt-based candidate generation from your prior work, is novel. This moves beyond treating retrieval as a flat list task to a structured reasoning problem.

proposals.title: InverseRAG: Generating Diverse and Challenging Queries from Wikipedia for RAG System Stress-Testing
proposals.motivation: The performance of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems is highly sensitive to the quality and diversity of user queries. Current evaluation benchmarks often rely on static, human-curated query sets, which may not fully expose systemic weaknesses like those identified in 'Lost in the Middle' contexts. Automatically generating a wide spectrum of queries—from simple fact extraction to complex, multi-hop reasoning—directly from the source corpus provides a scalable method for stress-testing retrieval and generation components under realistic, corpus-grounded conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: By fine-tuning a language model with a contrastive objective on (passage, query) pairs, we can generate queries that are not only relevant but also vary in cognitive demand and retrieval difficulty, thereby creating a more robust diagnostic benchmark for RAG systems than static datasets.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset from Wikipedia and existing QA benchmarks (e.g., Natural Questions) to create (passage, gold query) pairs. We will fine-tune a T5 or a decoder-only LM (like Llama 2) using a controlled text generation approach. The model will be prompted with a Wikipedia passage and a 'query type' control code (e.g., 'simple_fact', 'multi-hop', 'verification'). To ensure diversity and challenge, we will incorporate a contrastive loss that penalizes the generation of trivial queries and a retrieval simulation step that scores the generated query's ability to retrieve the source passage from a distracter set.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel methodology for automated, corpus-grounded query generation, 2) a new diagnostic benchmark of generated queries categorized by difficulty and type, and 3) an analysis of RAG failure modes exposed by this benchmark, extending findings on context utilization and retrieval sensitivity.
proposals.novelty: While query generation exists, our approach explicitly optimizes for query *difficulty* and *diagnostic value* for RAG systems, moving beyond simple relevance. It inverts the typical RAG evaluation process by using the source text to probe system weaknesses, a direct application of the 'inverse problem' perspective.

proposals.title: GENRE for the World: Evaluating and Enhancing Autoregressive Entity Retrieval in Multilingual Settings
proposals.motivation: Multilingual entity retrieval is crucial for cross-lingual information systems, yet dense retrieval methods often struggle with the rich morphological and semantic variations of entity names across languages. The autoregressive retrieval paradigm of GENRE (Generative ENtity REtrieval), which frames retrieval as sequence-to-sequence generation of entity names, offers a promising alternative. However, its performance and generalization capabilities beyond English-centric benchmarks, as highlighted by the researcher's prior work on model generalization and long-context use, remain underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the GENRE framework can be effectively adapted for robust multilingual entity retrieval, but its performance will be sensitive to language-specific surface form variations and the composition of its pre-training data, requiring targeted architectural or prompting adjustments.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic evaluation of the original GENRE model (and its successors like mGENRE) on established multilingual entity-linking datasets (e.g., MLWikM, XEL). Building on the researcher's expertise in prompt-based information extraction, we will design and test discrete prompting strategies to condition the model on language cues or entity type constraints. We will analyze failure modes, particularly for languages with complex morphology or lower resource representation, and explore fine-tuning strategies using multilingual Wikipedia as a knowledge source to improve cross-lingual alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of autoregressive retrieval for multilingual entities, identifying key challenges and opportunities. It will contribute a methodological analysis of prompting techniques for controlled generation in retrieval tasks and potentially release enhanced model checkpoints or prompting templates for improved cross-lingual performance.
proposals.novelty: While GENRE is established for English, its systematic application and analysis in a multilingual setting is novel. This work uniquely combines the autoregressive retrieval paradigm with a focused investigation of language-specific generalization, directly extending the researcher's lines of inquiry into prompt design and retrieval system performance.

proposals.title: Entity-Type-Enhanced Retrieval for Robustness in RAG Systems
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems often fail when retrieved passages contain entities with matching names but incorrect semantic types for the query (e.g., retrieving 'Apple' the company for a query about the fruit), degrading answer accuracy. Pre-2026 work, including the 'Lost in the Middle' phenomenon, shows that LLMs struggle to ignore irrelevant context, making high-precision retrieval critical. This project addresses a key bottleneck in RAG's reliability for entity-centric queries.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating coarse-grained entity type signals (e.g., PERSON, ORGANIZATION, LOCATION) into the dense retrieval and re-ranking stages will significantly improve the precision of candidate retrieval for fact-seeking queries, leading to more accurate and robust RAG system performance.
proposals.method: We will augment a dual-encoder retriever (e.g., DPR or Contriever) by concatenating a learned embedding of the predicted entity type(s) from the query to its dense representation. For re-ranking, we will integrate a similar type-matching signal as a feature into a cross-encoder (e.g., MonoT5) or use a lightweight adapter. Entity types will be extracted from queries and passages using a pre-2026 off-the-shelf tagger (like spaCy's or a Flair NER model) mapped to a coarse-grained set (e.g., Wikipedia's top-level categories). The system will be evaluated on entity-heavy QA benchmarks like WebQuestions and EntityQuestions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, lightweight method to inject structured semantic knowledge into neural retrieval, reducing semantic drift errors. It will yield a publicly available benchmark analysis of entity-type retrieval failures and a modular component easily integrable into existing RAG pipelines to improve their robustness.
proposals.novelty: While entity linking is well-studied, directly using coarse-grained types as a soft constraint within end-to-end trainable dense retrieval and re-ranking models is less explored. This approach is distinct from hard-filtering and more flexible than requiring a full knowledge graph.

proposals.title: DPR for RAG: A Systematic Study of Dense Passage Retrieval in Modern Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems critically depend on the candidate retrieval stage, yet many implementations rely on sparse retrieval (e.g., BM25) or simple, untuned dense embeddings. Recent advances in open-domain QA, such as Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) and its successors, have demonstrated superior performance by training retrievers on question-passage pairs, but their systematic integration and evaluation within end-to-end RAG pipelines remain underexplored. This gap limits the potential performance and robustness of RAG systems, especially for complex, multi-hop, or factually precise tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing sparse or generic dense retrieval with a task-specifically trained DPR-style retriever will significantly improve the end-to-end performance of RAG systems, particularly on tasks requiring high precision and the synthesis of information from multiple documents, by providing more relevant context to the generator.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled RAG framework where the retrieval component is modular. We will train and compare several dense retriever architectures (e.g., DPR, ANCE, ColBERT) using contrastive loss on question-answer pairs from datasets like Natural Questions and HotpotQA. The retrievers will be evaluated within the RAG pipeline using multiple generator LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-2, Flan-T5) on a suite of tasks including open-domain QA and fact verification. The core experiment will be an ablation study isolating the impact of the retrieval method on final answer accuracy, with analysis on metrics like retrieval recall@k and answer faithfulness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical benchmark for dense retrieval techniques within RAG, quantifying their benefit over traditional methods. It will yield practical guidance and potentially release optimized retriever checkpoints for RAG, advancing the engineering and understanding of hybrid retrieval-generation systems.
proposals.novelty: While dense retrieval is known in QA, its role in the full RAG pipeline—especially interacting with modern, long-context LLMs and its effect on mitigating issues like 'lost-in-the-middle' context utilization—has not been systematically studied, making its optimal configuration and performance gains non-obvious.

proposals.title: Contrastive Entity-Context Alignment (CECA): Improving Dense Retrieval for Entity-Centric RAG
proposals.motivation: Dense retrieval systems, particularly bi-encoders, are central to Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) but often underperform on fine-grained entity-centric queries. Pre-2026, entity embeddings are typically trained with pointwise or pairwise losses, which may not sufficiently capture the nuanced relationships between entities and their diverse textual contexts. This limits the precision of retrieval in knowledge-intensive tasks, a core challenge identified in RAG system analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training bi-encoders with a contrastive loss that explicitly aligns entities with their relevant Wikipedia descriptions and contexts, while pushing apart semantically distinct entities, will yield more discriminative embeddings and improve retrieval performance for entity-focused queries.
proposals.method: We will train a dual-tower transformer encoder (e.g., BERT-base) using a contrastive InfoNCE loss. Positive pairs will be (entity name, relevant Wikipedia passage containing its definition or key description). Hard negatives will be mined in-batch (other entities in the batch) and via BM25 from Wikipedia for semantically similar but distinct entities. We will evaluate on established entity linking and entity retrieval benchmarks (e.g., ZESHEL, ER-NQ), comparing against bi-encoders trained with standard cross-entropy loss and analyzing the impact on downstream RAG QA performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic evaluation of contrastive learning for entity embedding in retrieval systems, demonstrating measurable gains in precision for entity-centric queries. It will contribute a publicly available model and analysis of how improved entity retrieval mitigates the 'lost-in-the-middle' problem in RAG by ensuring more relevant facts are retrieved.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in vision and sentence representation, its targeted application to improve entity embeddings for dense retrieval, specifically within the RAG framework and using structured Wikipedia data, is a novel synthesis of Petroni's prior work on information extraction and retrieval's role in RAG systems.

proposals.title: BinEmb: Scaling Cross-Architecture Binary Code Embeddings with Massive Multi-Source Corpora
proposals.motivation: The analysis of binary code is crucial for security (e.g., vulnerability detection, malware analysis) and software engineering (e.g., patch analysis, code reuse). However, existing methods for creating semantic embeddings of binaries are limited by small, homogeneous training datasets, hindering their generalization across diverse architectures, compilers, and optimization levels. Scaling to massive, diverse binary corpora, a frontier unexplored before 2026, is necessary to build robust, general-purpose binary retrieval and analysis systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Training a transformer-based embedding model on an unprecedented scale and diversity of disassembled binary functions will produce embeddings that generalize effectively across compilation contexts, enabling high-accuracy semantic retrieval and similarity tasks for binary code.
proposals.method: We will construct a corpus of billions of functions from binaries across architectures (x86, ARM, MIPS), compilers (GCC, Clang, MSVC), optimization levels, and software domains (OS kernels, libraries, applications). We will disassemble binaries using tools like Ghidra, representing functions as sequences of normalized assembly mnemonics and operands. A BERT-style transformer encoder will be trained using a contrastive learning objective (e.g., SimCSE or InfoNCE) on pairs of semantically equivalent functions compiled under different settings, mined from the corpus. The model's performance will be evaluated on retrieval tasks (finding similar functions) and cross-compilation generalization benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first general-purpose embedding model for binary code trained at a web-scale, along with a massive open dataset for benchmarking.
