It will advance the state-of-the-art in binary code similarity detection and establish a foundational retrieval component for future Binary RAG systems, directly extending Petroni's work on IR's role in RAG to the low-code domain.
proposals.novelty: While NLP embeddings are trained on trillion-token corpora, binary embedding research pre-2026 uses orders-of-magnitude less data. This proposal applies the 'scale is all you need' paradigm from NLP to the structured, discrete domain of assembly language, tackling the unique challenge of creating semantic invariance across compiler-induced syntactic variations.

proposals.title: Beyond Wikipedia: A Multi-Source Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Enhanced Factual Coverage and Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems predominantly rely on Wikipedia as a single, monolithic knowledge source, which introduces coverage gaps and potential bias. This limitation is critical as real-world queries require information from diverse domains like scientific literature, news, and structured databases. A systematic investigation into integrating multiple, specialized knowledge sources is needed to improve the factual grounding and robustness of language models.
proposals.hypothesis: A RAG system that dynamically retrieves from multiple, specialized knowledge sources (e.g., academic abstracts, news archives, knowledge graphs) will achieve higher factual accuracy and better reasoning performance on complex, open-domain tasks than a system relying solely on Wikipedia.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-source RAG architecture with a router that classifies queries to select the most appropriate source(s) from a pre-defined set (e.g., PubMed, Common Crawl news, Wikidata). For retrieval, we will implement and compare a unified dense index against source-specific retrievers. The generation component will be prompted to synthesize evidence from the retrieved, potentially conflicting, passages. Evaluation will use existing QA benchmarks (e.g., HotpotQA, FEVER) and a new test set requiring cross-source reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a blueprint for building heterogeneous-knowledge RAG systems and a rigorous analysis of the trade-offs between unified and specialized retrieval. It will also release a benchmark for evaluating multi-source factual reasoning, advancing the field beyond single-source retrieval paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied retrieval from large corpora, this proposal explicitly focuses on the orchestration and fusion of *structurally and topically distinct* knowledge sources, moving beyond the de facto standard of Wikipedia to address its inherent coverage limitations.

proposals.title: Parametric Factual Update: A Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning Framework for Dynamic Knowledge
proposals.motivation: Current RAG systems rely on a strict parametric/non-parametric memory split, where static parametric knowledge is supplemented by a retrievable external corpus. This limits efficiency and coherence, as models cannot directly update their core factual understanding post-training. As shown in 'Lost in the Middle' and IR-RAG research, effectively using long, retrieved contexts remains a challenge, suggesting a need for more integrated knowledge management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted, retrieval-augmented fine-tuning process can enable parametric memory to directly absorb and update factual knowledge from a curated non-parametric store, thereby improving the accuracy and latency of factual recall without catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage process. First, we will use discrete information extraction prompts (inspired by Petroni's prior work) to identify and extract high-confidence factual tuples (subject, relation, object) from a dynamic Wikipedia snapshot, creating a 'knowledge diff' from the model's training data. Second, we will fine-tune a base language model (e.g., LLaMA-2) on sequences that interleave these new facts with their original parametric counterparts, using a modified loss that strongly weights the new factual tuples while employing regularization (e.g., EWC or replay) to preserve general task performance. The system's efficacy will be evaluated on a temporal QA benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for targeted parametric knowledge updates, challenging the rigid RAG dichotomy. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility and limits of factual editing in medium-scale models and deliver a framework for more efficient, hybrid knowledge systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard RAG or full model retraining, this approach directly targets the parametric knowledge update problem using a retrieval-driven fine-tuning paradigm, explicitly aiming to merge the strengths of both memory types. It moves beyond prompting for extraction to using extraction for guided model editing.

proposals.title: Beyond QA: A Systematic Evaluation of RAG for Knowledge-Intensive Dialogue and Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current RAG research is heavily focused on question-answering, creating a narrow view of its capabilities and failure modes. However, real-world applications like conversational agents and document summarization are equally knowledge-intensive but impose different structural and coherence demands on generation. A systematic evaluation across these diverse tasks is needed to understand RAG's general utility and to guide the development of more robust, task-agnostic retrieval-augmented systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of RAG systems degrades significantly on open-ended generation tasks like dialogue and summarization compared to QA, due to challenges in integrating multiple retrieved passages into a coherent, long-form output, and that task-specific retrieval and fusion strategies can mitigate this degradation.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark from existing datasets (e.g., Wizard of Wikipedia for dialogue, PubMed or arXiv for summarization) to evaluate standard RAG pipelines. We will analyze failure modes linked to context length and position effects, extending insights from 'Lost in the Middle'. Building on Petroni's work on prompt generalization, we will design and test discrete prompts to guide the LM in selectively attending to and synthesizing retrieved evidence for each task. The core technical comparison will be between a standard 'retrieve-then-read' architecture and a novel iterative retrieval process where initial generation triggers follow-up queries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, empirical analysis of RAG for major generation tasks beyond QA, identifying key bottlenecks. It will yield a public benchmark and propose actionable, task-aware prompting strategies for improving retrieval integration. The findings will inform the design of next-generation RAG systems for real-world applications.
proposals.novelty: While RAG for QA is well-studied, a controlled, diagnostic evaluation across structurally different generation tasks is novel. The approach uniquely combines analysis of long-context weaknesses with prompt-based control mechanisms specifically tailored for dialogue and summarization, moving beyond one-size-fits-all retrieval fusion.

proposals.title: Scaling Non-Parametric Memory to the Web: A Distributed Architecture for Trillion-Token Retrieval-Augmented Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are fundamentally limited by the scale of their non-parametric memory, typically constrained to single-database indices. To achieve truly comprehensive knowledge grounding, as envisioned in systems like WebGPT, we must index corpora on the scale of the entire web. This requires novel distributed retrieval architectures that maintain low-latency, high-recall performance, a critical challenge pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, task-routed distributed retrieval system, which decomposes queries and selectively queries specialized sub-indices, can efficiently access a trillion-token corpus while outperforming monolithic index baselines in both accuracy and latency.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture: a router model, trained via prompt-based instruction following, classifies queries into domains (e.g., science, history). It then directs queries to a federation of specialized, pre-existing vector indices (e.g., Wikipedia, arXiv, Common Crawl chunks) built using FAISS or SCaNN. A final cross-index re-ranker, informed by the 'Lost in the Middle' phenomenon, will optimally fuse and re-order evidence. We will evaluate on massively multi-hop QA tasks constructed from the Pile and Natural Questions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a blueprint and open-source framework for web-scale RAG, demonstrating how to orchestrate existing indices. It will yield new insights into the performance trade-offs of distributed vs. centralized retrieval and establish benchmarks for trillion-token knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales single indices, we uniquely treat the web as a federation of heterogeneous, pre-indexed resources. Our router-based approach for query decomposition and resource selection moves beyond simple brute-force search, addressing the systems challenge of efficient access at unprecedented scale.

proposals.title: Inverse Retrieval: Training a Query Generation Model for Document Retrieval Analysis
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems critically depend on the quality of the initial document retrieval, yet we lack robust methods to analyze and understand the query-document relationship from the document's perspective. This inverse problem—predicting which query would retrieve a given passage—can provide a diagnostic lens for retrieval systems, revealing biases and failure modes in dense retrievers and sparse lexical systems. Understanding this mapping is essential for improving RAG robustness and for generating synthetic training data to enhance retrieval models.
proposals.hypothesis: A model trained to generate plausible retrieval queries from document passages can learn a generalized representation of the query-document relevance function, and the characteristics of its generated queries can serve as a diagnostic tool for analyzing retrieval system performance and biases.
proposals.method: We will train a sequence-to-sequence T5 or FLAN-T5 model, framing the task as conditional text generation. Training data will be constructed from existing query-passage relevance pairs, such as those from MS MARCO or Natural Questions, where the passage is the input and the query is the target output. We will experiment with discrete prompting strategies to condition the generator on the specific retrieval system (e.g., BM25, DPR, Contriever) whose behavior we aim to invert. The model's performance will be evaluated by using its generated queries to retrieve the original document, measuring the recall rate across different retrievers.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel diagnostic framework for retrieval systems by inverting the query-document relationship. It will provide a method to generate synthetic, system-tailored query data for retriever training or augmentation. The findings will offer new insights into how different retrievers interpret document relevance, complementing Petroni's prior work on RAG systems and information extraction generalization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on generating queries for data augmentation, our approach explicitly frames query generation as an inverse retrieval problem to diagnose and model the behavior of specific retrieval systems. This system-conditioned inversion for analysis, rather than just data creation, is a distinct and unexplored angle.

proposals.title: Inverse RAG: Automating Question Generation for Indexed Document Coverage
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system performance is critically dependent on the quality of its document index. However, pre-2026 evaluation often relies on static, human-written QA pairs, which may not comprehensively probe the index's factual coverage or expose retrieval blind spots. Automatically generating diverse, document-grounded questions offers a scalable method for stress-testing and improving index construction, directly addressing the 'coverage' challenge highlighted in recent RAG analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs), when guided by structured prompts and the content of indexed documents, can generate high-quality, answerable questions that effectively surface gaps in document retrieval coverage and, when used for iterative refinement, can lead to more robust RAG systems.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage pipeline. First, we adapt few-shot prompting techniques, informed by Petroni's work on discrete information extraction prompts, to instruct an LLM (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2) to generate questions for key factual claims within a given document chunk. Second, we employ these generated questions in a retrieval evaluation loop: we query the index and measure answer recall, identifying poorly retrieved document segments. We will experiment with iterative refinement, where 'missed' segments are used to generate further adversarial questions or to guide document re-chunking strategies, mitigating 'lost-in-the-middle' effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, automated framework for evaluating and diagnosing RAG index quality beyond static benchmarks. It will yield a methodology for generating targeted, in-domain QA data to improve retrieval systems and offer new insights into the relationship between document representation and query-ability.
proposals.novelty: While question generation is studied, its systematic application as an *inverse problem* for RAG index auditing and iterative improvement is novel. This approach directly inverts the standard RAG workflow (query->retrieve->answer) to proactively generate queries that test retrieval boundaries.

proposals.title: Decoupling Factual Knowledge from Model Weights: A Retrieval-Augmented Architecture for Dynamic Knowledge Encoding
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) encode factual knowledge statically within their parameters, leading to issues of obsolescence, inefficiency in updating knowledge, and difficulty in provenance verification. This static paradigm, as highlighted by the 'Lost in the Middle' problem and RAG research, is at odds with the dynamic nature of real-world information, creating a fundamental bottleneck for reliable and transparent knowledge-intensive tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural architecture can be designed where a core reasoning model with fixed, task-oriented parameters is systematically and dynamically augmented by a separate, updatable knowledge encoder, leading to more accurate, current, and traceable factual outputs than a statically parameterized model of comparable size.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component system: a compact, frozen 'reasoning engine' (a pre-trained LM) and a trainable 'knowledge encoder' network. The encoder will process retrieved evidence chunks (e.g., from Wikipedia) into dense vector representations using a method like ColBERTv2. A novel cross-attention adapter will be inserted between transformer layers of the frozen LM, allowing it to condition its next-token predictions directly on these dynamic encoder outputs. We will train the encoder and adapter end-to-end on QA and fact verification tasks, using prompts to guide the retrieval and integration process.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architectural blueprint for separating reasoning capacity from factual memory in LMs. It will provide empirical evidence on the performance trade-offs between static and dynamic knowledge encoding, and offer a new framework for evaluating model knowledge that emphasizes retrieval-augmented accuracy and provenance over parametric recall.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) which treats the LM as a black-box reader, our approach integrates retrieved knowledge deeply into the model's internal computation via a trainable pathway, moving beyond simple prompt concatenation. It also differs from continual learning approaches that update all weights, by strictly isolating and specializing components for dynamic knowledge.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Retrieval-Augmentation for Abstractive Language Generation Tasks
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has proven effective for knowledge-intensive tasks like open-domain QA, but its application to abstractive tasks like summarization and dialogue remains heavily reliant on supervised retrieval training or curated knowledge bases. This limits scalability and adaptability. An unsupervised method, which retrieves based on the raw language model's own predictions (as in the REALM or Atlas cloze-task paradigm), could enable more flexible and efficient RAG systems for a broader range of applications without task-specific retrieval supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: The core unsupervised retrieval-augmentation principle—where a language model's own pre-training objective (e.g., masked language modeling) guides the retrieval of relevant context—can be effectively adapted and generalized to guide retrieval for abstractive generation tasks, leading to improved factual consistency and relevance in summaries and dialogues without task-specific retrieval fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the unsupervised method from cloze-QA, where a masked query derived from the input guides retrieval from a corpus like Wikipedia. For summarization, we will generate a set of potential factual 'claims' or key phrases from the source document using the LM, mask them, and use them as queries. For dialogue, we will use the dialogue history to generate masked queries about likely needed world knowledge. The retrieved passages will be prepended as context for a sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only LM). We will rigorously evaluate on benchmarks like CNN/Daily Mail (summarization) and Wizard of Wikipedia (dialogue), measuring factual accuracy (via QA-based metrics) and standard generation metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework and empirical evidence for applying unsupervised RAG to abstractive tasks, reducing the need for supervised retrieval components. It will yield new insights into how LMs can self-guide knowledge retrieval and contribute a more general, cost-effective RAG paradigm for the community.
proposals.novelty: While unsupervised RAG is established for cloze tasks, its extension to open-ended generation is non-trivial due to the lack of a clear, single 'answer' to guide retrieval. The novelty lies in designing mechanisms to derive effective unsupervised retrieval signals from the source text or context for these fundamentally different task formulations.

proposals.title: Scaling Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Systematic Performance Analysis for Large Autoregressive Language Models
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a critical paradigm for grounding large language models (LLMs) in external knowledge, but foundational RAG research has primarily focused on encoder-based models like BERT or smaller-scale autoregressive models. As the field shifts towards deploying massive, GPT-style LLMs in production systems, it is unclear how RAG performance scales with model size and architecture. Understanding this relationship is essential for building efficient, high-performance knowledge-intensive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance gains from retrieval-augmentation for knowledge-intensive tasks follow a non-linear relationship with autoregressive LLM scale, where benefits diminish beyond a certain model size due to increased parametric knowledge, but that optimal integration strategies differ significantly from those used with encoder-based models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled experiment using a suite of knowledge-intensive QA tasks (e.g., Natural Questions, TriviaQA). We will apply a standardized RAG pipeline—using a dense retriever (Contriever or DPR) over Wikipedia—to a series of decoder-only LLMs of varying scales (e.g., GPT-2, GPT-NeoX, OPT, and the LLaMA family). We will systematically test different prompting strategies for integrating retrieved passages, analyzing metrics like answer accuracy, context utilization (inspired by 'Lost in the Middle' analysis), and sensitivity to retrieval noise. The core comparison will be RAG-augmented performance vs. the model's zero-shot/few-shot capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence on how RAG scales with modern LLM size, offering practical guidance for system designers on when retrieval is necessary. It will contribute a benchmark analysis of integration prompts for large autoregressive models and potentially reveal task-model size sweet spots for cost-effective RAG deployment.
proposals.novelty: While RAG for BERT-class models is well-studied and RAG is used with LLMs, a rigorous, controlled study isolating the effect of autoregressive model scale on RAG efficacy is novel. It directly tests whether the core assumptions of RAG hold for models with vast internal knowledge.

proposals.title: Corpus Provenance Matters: A Systematic Study of Retrieval Corpus Impact on Factual Prediction in RAG
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a dominant paradigm for grounding language models in external knowledge, yet its performance is assumed to be heavily dependent on the retrieval corpus. Prior work, including Petroni's on RAG systems and long-context usage, has focused on retrieval methods and model architectures, but lacks a controlled, systematic analysis of how the inherent properties of the retrieval corpus itself—such as domain, granularity, and veracity—affect downstream factual accuracy. Understanding this is crucial for building reliable, high-performance knowledge-intensive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the factual prediction performance of a RAG system is non-monotonically sensitive to corpus characteristics, where performance peaks with a corpus of intermediate domain specificity and high factual density, and that the impact varies significantly across different types of factual queries (e.g., entity-centric vs. relational).
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled testbed using established fact verification datasets (e.g., FEVER) and open-domain QA tasks. We will pair them with multiple, distinct retrieval corpora, including general (Wikipedia), domain-specific (e.g., PubMed, arXiv), synthetic (generated by an LLM), and noisy web-mined corpora. Using a fixed, state-of-the-art retriever (e.g., Contriever) and a fixed LLM (e.g., Llama 2 or GPT-3.5-Turbo via API), we will run a full factorial experiment, measuring end-task performance while instrumenting the pipeline to log retrieval precision/recall and the model's attention to the provided context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, empirical framework for evaluating retrieval corpus suitability, yielding actionable guidelines for corpus selection in RAG deployment. It will contribute a diagnostic benchmark to disentangle retrieval quality from corpus quality effects, advancing the systematic engineering of robust knowledge systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work varies retrievers and models, the systematic isolation and variation of the retrieval corpus as the primary independent variable is novel. This moves beyond the assumption that 'more data is better' to a nuanced understanding of corpus-provenance effects.

proposals.title: Dense Retrieval for RAG: A Systematic Transfer from Information Retrieval to Improve Context Matching
proposals.motivation: Current RAG systems often rely on sparse, keyword-based retrievers (e.g., BM25) which can fail to capture semantic relevance, leading to poor context selection for the generator. This mismatch is a critical bottleneck, as highlighted by recent analyses of RAG systems and the 'lost in the middle' problem in LLMs. Transferring mature dense retrieval techniques from the Information Retrieval (IR) field offers a promising path to more accurate and robust retrieval-augmented generation.
proposals.hypothesis: Transferring and adapting state-of-the-art dense retrieval models (e.g., DPR, ANCE, ColBERT) from standalone IR tasks to the RAG pipeline will significantly improve the quality of retrieved context over sparse methods, leading to measurable gains in downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will systematically benchmark pre-trained dense retrievers (like Contriever) and IR-optimized models (like ColBERT) against BM25 baselines within a standard RAG framework on knowledge-intensive QA tasks (e.g., Natural Questions). The retriever will be evaluated both frozen and fine-tuned end-to-end with the LLM generator (e.g., T5, Llama 2). We will analyze retrieval metrics (recall@k, MRR) and final answer accuracy, with a specific focus on how retrieval quality mitigates issues like 'lost in the middle' context utilization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical foundation for selecting and integrating dense retrievers into RAG, moving beyond heuristic sparse retrieval. It will yield a publicly available benchmark suite and establish best practices for retriever-generator alignment, directly addressing the retrieval challenges outlined in prior RAG analyses.
proposals.novelty: While dense retrieval is established in IR, its systematic transfer and ablation within the complete RAG loop—especially analyzing the interaction between retriever semantics and generator context usage patterns—remains underexplored compared to prompt engineering or generator-focused work.

proposals.title: Multi-Hop RAG: An Iterative Retrieval and Reasoning Framework for Complex Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Current RAG systems, as analyzed in IR-RAG @ SIGIR24, excel at single-fact retrieval but struggle with multi-hop reasoning questions requiring synthesis across multiple documents. This is a critical limitation for real-world applications like historical analysis or scientific literature review, where answers are not contained in a single passage. The 'Lost in the Middle' phenomenon further complicates this, as models fail to effectively use all retrieved contexts when they are numerous or long.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative RAG system that decomposes complex questions into a sequence of single-hop retrieval and reasoning steps, guided by discrete information extraction prompts, will outperform monolithic single-retrieval RAG on multi-hop QA tasks by improving both retrieval precision and the language model's ability to synthesize information.
proposals.method: We propose a controlled, iterative framework. First, a prompt-based query decomposer (inspired by discrete prompt generalization work) breaks the initial question into sub-questions. A retriever fetches evidence for the first sub-question from Wikipedia. A reader LM answers it, and its output is used to reformulate the next query, repeating until a final answer generator synthesizes the chain. We will experiment with different prompting strategies for decomposition and synthesis, and evaluate on established multi-hop benchmarks like HotpotQA and 2WikiMultihopQA, comparing against strong single-retrieval RAG baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic analysis of the failure modes of standard RAG on multi-hop tasks and deliver a robust, interpretable framework for complex QA. It will contribute new insights into the interaction between iterative retrieval and reasoning, and release a benchmark suite for evaluating multi-hop RAG performance.
proposals.novelty: While iterative retrieval exists, our approach uniquely integrates Petroni's work on the generalizability of discrete prompts to control the decomposition and reasoning steps, creating a more transparent and tunable process than end-to-end learned methods prevalent before 2026.

proposals.title: Injecting Structured Knowledge into Language Models via Constrained Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Research often treats language models (LMs) as implicit knowledge bases (KBs) to be evaluated against structured KBs like Wikidata. However, this comparison overlooks the potential for structured KBs to actively improve LMs. Given known LM limitations in factual recall, reasoning, and handling long contexts, directly integrating the precision and relational structure of KBs could significantly enhance LM performance and reliability for knowledge-intensive tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Fine-tuning a language model with a retrieval mechanism that is explicitly constrained and supervised by a structured knowledge base will lead to more accurate, consistent, and efficient knowledge utilization compared to standard pre-training or generic RAG approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a fine-tuning framework where, for each training example, a retrieval module queries a target KB (e.g., Wikidata) for relevant subgraphs. A constraint module, using discrete prompts or learned classifiers, filters and structures this information into a formal, model-readable input (e.g., a linearized set of triples). The LM is then trained to generate the target output conditioned on this structured prompt. We will experiment with constrained attention mechanisms to bias the model towards the retrieved facts and evaluate on fact-centric QA, entity linking, and relation extraction tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel methodology for KB-guided LM fine-tuning and an empirical analysis of how structured knowledge injection affects LM capabilities. It will provide insights into mitigating hallucination and improving reasoning precision, advancing the design of more reliable, knowledge-grounded language systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses KBs only for evaluation or employs them as a passive retrieval corpus in RAG, this approach actively uses the KB's schema and constraints to supervise and structure the LM's training process, inverting the typical LM-as-KB comparison paradigm.

proposals.title: KG-Metric: Evaluating Factual Recall in Language Models with Knowledge Graph Embedding Metrics
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the factual knowledge of large language models (LLMs) is crucial for their safe and reliable deployment. Current methods, like simple accuracy on QA datasets, are coarse-grained and fail to capture the relational structure and semantic nuance of factual errors. Knowledge graph (KG) completion has a mature suite of embedding-based metrics (e.g., Hits@k, MRR) that precisely measure how well a model understands entity relationships, offering a more systematic evaluation framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying KG embedding evaluation metrics to LLM factual recall tasks will provide a more granular, diagnostic, and semantically meaningful assessment of model performance than traditional accuracy, revealing specific relational weaknesses (e.g., confusion between symmetric and asymmetric relations) that are currently obscured.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark by sampling factual triples (head, relation, tail) from Wikidata. For each triple, we will prompt LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-2, GPT-3.5) to generate the tail entity given the head and relation, framing it as a cloze-style task. Instead of just checking for exact match, we will treat the LLM's probability distribution over the vocabulary as a scoring function for candidate tail entities. We will then compute standard KG completion metrics—Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) and Hits@{1,3,10}—on these scored rankings, directly comparing LLM performance to traditional KG embedding models like TransE and ComplEx.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a standardized, fine-grained evaluation suite for LLM factual knowledge, bridging the methodologies of NLP and knowledge representation. It will produce diagnostic insights into the types of relations LLMs struggle with, informing future model development and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) strategies. The resulting benchmark and metrics will be released to the community.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., 'Lost in the Middle') analyzes context usage, and Petroni et al. introduced prompt-based probing, directly porting the rigorous ranking-based evaluation paradigm from knowledge graph completion to autoregressive LLM factual recall is novel. This approach moves beyond binary right/wrong judgments to a continuous, relational assessment of knowledge.

proposals.title: Architectural Agnosticism of Bias-Only Tuning: A Systematic Evaluation Across Transformer Variants
proposals.motivation: Bias-only fine-tuning, where only the bias parameters of a language model are updated, has emerged as a highly parameter-efficient adaptation method. However, its effectiveness has been demonstrated primarily on standard decoder-only Transformer architectures (e.g., GPT-style models). It remains unknown whether this efficiency generalizes to other prevalent architectures, such as encoder-decoder models (T5) and encoder-only models (BERT), which have different structural biases and parameter distributions. Establishing the scope of this method's applicability is crucial for developing efficient, architecture-agnostic adaptation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness of bias-only fine-tuning is not uniform across architectures; its performance will correlate with the architectural role and distribution of bias parameters within different model families (e.g., decoder-only vs. encoder-decoder).
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study, applying bias-only fine-tuning to a suite of pre-trained models including decoder-only (LLaMA-2, GPT-2), encoder-decoder (T5, FLAN-T5), and encoder-only (BERT, RoBERTa) architectures. Using a fixed set of information extraction and question-answering tasks from Petroni's prior work, we will compare bias-only tuning against full fine-tuning and other parameter-efficient methods (e.g., LoRA). We will analyze performance trends, convergence speed, and the relationship between task performance and the norm of updated bias vectors across layers specific to each architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of bias-only tuning efficacy across the major Transformer architecture families, offering practical guidance for model selection and efficient adaptation. It will contribute a deeper understanding of how architectural differences influence parameter-efficient learning, potentially leading to more tailored lightweight fine-tuning protocols.
proposals.novelty: While bias-only tuning is known, its systematic evaluation is confined to autoregressive LMs. Extending this analysis to encoder-decoder and encoder-only models, which are fundamental to retrieval and classification tasks, is a novel and necessary step to assess the method's general utility.

proposals.title: Generalizing Null-Prompt Finetuning: A Systematic Evaluation Across Diverse NLP Tasks
proposals.motivation: Recent work on 'null-prompt' finetuning, where models are trained to respond to an empty or generic prompt, has shown promise for improving robustness and generalization in specific information extraction tasks. However, its efficacy and impact on model behavior across a broader spectrum of NLP tasks—such as reasoning, generation, and long-context understanding—remain unexplored. Given the community's push towards more efficient and generalizable adaptation methods, a systematic evaluation is crucial to understand the method's true scope and limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that null-prompt finetuning will enhance task generalization and robustness for a range of NLP tasks beyond information extraction, but its effectiveness will be modulated by task complexity and the model's inherent ability to leverage long-range context.
proposals.method: We will adapt the null-prompt finetuning protocol, training models like T5 or Llama-2 on a diverse benchmark suite (e.g., BIG-Bench Hard, MMLU, and long-context QA from QuALITY) using a generic trigger like 'Task:'. We will compare performance against standard instruction-tuning and few-shot prompting baselines. The evaluation will include standard accuracy, robustness to prompt perturbations, and an analysis of attention patterns to investigate changes in information retrieval within long contexts, linking to findings from 'Lost in the Middle'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of null-prompt finetuning's capabilities and failure modes across NLP, offering practical guidance for its application. It will contribute a nuanced understanding of how minimalist prompting interacts with model architecture to affect task performance and internal retrieval dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While null-prompting has been studied for extraction, its application to a broad, challenging benchmark suite integrating reasoning and long-context tasks is novel. The analysis linking performance changes to internal retrieval mechanisms extends beyond mere benchmarking to explanatory insight.

ideas.title: Dual: use entity linking to improve retrieval models
ideas.description: Use the entity linking system to generate hard training data for improving dense passage retrieval.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Investigate retrieval-augmentation for larger LMs
ideas.description: Test the method on language models larger than BERT, like GPT-style architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.title: Variable-Length Salient Phrase Inversion for Position-Agnostic Memorization
ideas.description: This approach trains models by deliberately placing the most information-rich, variable-length phrases (e.g., key concepts, named entities, or technical definitions) in non-standard sequence positions, such as the middle, while less informative content occupies the beginnings and ends. The model must learn to identify these salient phrases based on their intrinsic semantic content and structure, not their positional embedding or fixed ngram context. This combines the positional inversion challenge with a more natural, phrase-based unit of meaning, pushing the model towards developing content-addressable memory mechanisms. The result is a model more robust to how information is structured in context, improving generalization on tasks where key data appears in unpredictable locations.

ideas.title: Use knowledge graph embedding metrics
ideas.description: Evaluate LM factual recall using metrics from knowledge graph completion tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Language Models as Knowledge Bases?

ideas.title: Assume tasks require textual generation
ideas.description: Explore models that output structured data or actions instead of disambiguated text.
ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Apply method to broader set of NLP tasks
ideas.description: Evaluate the null-prompt finetuning approach on a more diverse and challenging benchmark suite.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cutting Down on Prompts and Parameters: Simple Few-Shot Learning with Language Models

ideas.title: Jointly-Optimized Latent Indexing for Dynamic Context Management
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond static database techniques by creating a trainable indexing layer that is co-optimized with the language model's task objective. A small encoder network generates latent pointers for context chunks, which are stored in a searchable structure that supports fast, approximate nearest-neighbor queries based on semantic similarity. During generation, the model can query this index with the current hidden state to retrieve the most relevant prior context, enabling efficient access to long-range dependencies. The entire system—encoder, index, and the core LM—is trained end-to-end, ensuring the index captures precisely the contextual features needed for downstream performance.

ideas.title: Retrodictive Query Generation via Inverse Problem Solving
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem-solving approach from geophysics and seismology to the task of generating queries from documents. In seismic tomography, scientists start with observed data (seismic waves) and work backward to infer the hidden structure that caused it. Analogously, we treat a document as the observable outcome and aim to infer the latent user intents or queries that would lead to it. The adapted research idea involves developing a model that inverts the standard document retrieval function. Instead of mapping a query to relevant documents, the system learns a generative model that, given a document, produces a distribution of plausible user queries. This could be achieved by training a neural model on query-document pairs to learn the conditional probability P(query|document), effectively solving the 'inverse' of the typical retrieval task. This method is useful for dataset augmentation, understanding document coverage from a user perspective, and improving retrieval systems by explicitly modeling the query generation process.

ideas.title: Apply dense passage retrieval techniques
ideas.description: Use advanced dense retrieval methods from open-domain QA for the candidate retrieval stage.
ideas.sourceTitle: Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Wikipedia is the sole knowledge source

ideas.sourceTitle: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

ideas.title: Challenge need for explicit retrieval

ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Massively scale the non-parametric memory
ideas.description: Index the entire web or vast corpora, requiring efficient distributed retrieval.
ideas.sourceTitle: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

ideas.title: Scalable Analysis of Positional Bias in Trillion-Token Training
ideas.description: This research fuses the study of training data effects on context usage with the engineering challenge of scaling to trillion-token corpora. The core objective is to develop a distributed experimental framework that can systematically vary the length, structure, and positional distribution of information within massive training datasets (e.g., by constructing tailored trillion-token mixtures of books, code, and long-form web text). By training models at this scale while instrumenting them to measure positional attention and information retrieval accuracy, the project will uncover how exposure to vast amounts of long-context data alters fundamental architectural behaviors, such as recency versus primacy bias or the ability to synthesize information from distant segments. This creates a new paradigm for data-centric scaling research, moving beyond simple loss curves to understand the emergent positional dynamics of models trained on internet-scale corpora.

ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.title: Candidate retrieval is always necessary

ideas.sourceTitle: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Curriculum-Driven Bidirectional Query-Document Synthesis
ideas.description: This approach integrates curriculum learning with bidirectional query-document generation to create a systematic training framework. A model is first trained to generate simple queries for the most salient document sections (e.g., beginnings and ends), then progressively learns to formulate queries for more complex, central document concepts. This synthesized capability allows the system to autonomously create a structured, difficulty-ranked dataset of (query, document) pairs, starting from a small seed corpus. The resulting curriculum can then be used to train or fine-tune other models, such as retrievers or QA systems, on a logically sequenced learning path that mirrors educational principles.

ideas.title: Scale to multi-hop reasoning tasks
ideas.description: Extend the system to answer questions requiring synthesis of information from multiple retrieved contexts.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.title: Extend unsupervised retrieval-augmentation to other LM tasks
ideas.description: Apply the unsupervised retrieval-augmentation method to tasks beyond cloze QA, like summarization or dialogue.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.title: Adapt Outward-Inward Sequencing from Archaeological Excavation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from archaeological excavation techniques, where diggers proceed from the outer edges of a site inward to preserve context and protect a central artifact, this idea adapts that 'outward-inward' sequencing to machine learning curriculum design. The analogous archaeological solution involves first clearing and documenting surrounding layers to establish stability before exposing the fragile core. Adapted to research, this translates to a training curriculum where models are first trained exclusively on data that highlights contextual boundaries and peripheral information (e.g., sentence delimiters, graph leaves), then gradually introduced to examples containing the complex, interdependent information from the 'middle' of the data structure. This method aims to build a model's understanding of overall structure and context before it learns to interpret the most semantically dense and ambiguous central components, potentially improving generalization and robustness on structured prediction tasks.

ideas.description: Integrate coarse-grained entity type signals to improve candidate retrieval and ranking.
ideas.sourceTitle: Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Use knowledge distillation for identifier scoring
ideas.description: Distill the scoring function of a large retriever model into a smaller, faster autoregressive model for ngram generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Autoregressive Search Engines: Generating Substrings as Document Identifiers

ideas.title: Use reinforcement learning for retrieval
ideas.description: Train the retriever with RL rewards based on downstream task success.
ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Learned Feature Selection for N-grams via Sparse Regularization and Gating
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from computational neuroscience, specifically neural encoding models used to identify sparse, predictive stimulus features. The analogous problem is determining which specific features of a complex sensory input (like a visual scene) drive a neuron's response, solved using sparse regularization or learned gating to select only the most informative features. Adapted to n-gram selection, we propose training a model (e.g., a neural network for document representation or retrieval) that incorporates a sparsity-inducing penalty on n-gram importance weights or a differentiable gating layer. This model would learn to dynamically select or emphasize a sparse subset of n-grams that are most distinctive for a document's identity or most relevant to a query, moving beyond the use of all n-grams and improving efficiency and discriminative power.

ideas.title: Adaptive Latent Indexing with Positional Bias Correction
ideas.description: This research direction proposes building latent indexing structures (like B-trees or learned indexes) over long context windows, but where the index's design and query routing are explicitly aware of the transformer's U-shaped attention bias (where middle positions are prioritized). The index would learn to place critical reference pointers or summaries in context regions the model finds 'difficult' (like the very beginning or end), effectively using the index to guide the model's attention. This creates a synergistic loop: the index optimizes information placement based on the model's bias, and the model, by relying on the index, achieves more uniform performance across the entire context, overcoming its native positional limitations.

ideas.title: Fuzzy-Tolerant Positional Inversion for Robust Sequence Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training sequence models on data where both the content and the expected position of key information are perturbed. The model's input sequences are subjected to fuzzy string edits (typos, synonyms, morphological variations) while the critical information for a task is systematically moved away from the model's default 'easy' region (e.g., the middle) toward the ends. This dual-perturbation forces the model to develop search mechanisms that are tolerant to surface-form variations *and* to dynamically re-weight positional attention based on content cues, rather than relying on static positional priors. The outcome is a more robust architecture that can handle noisy, real-world data where important signals are both lexically varied and irregularly placed within a sequence.

ideas.title: Knowledge is only in non-parametric memory
ideas.description: Challenge the split by making parametric memory also updatable or factual.
ideas.sourceTitle: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

ideas.title: Communication-Aware Hierarchical Architectures for Trillion-Token Model Training and Inference
ideas.description: This research investigates a fundamental redesign of distributed systems for trillion-token corpora, prioritizing the minimization of communication overhead as the primary constraint. The approach co-designs a hierarchical index structure with model parallelism, ensuring that frequently accessed data and corresponding model parameters are collocated within high-bandwidth compute pods. It develops novel asynchronous synchronization and compressed communication primitives specifically for the dynamic, retrieval-augmented workflows of large language models, enabling feasible training and low-latency inference at unprecedented scale by making the communication cost sub-linear in corpus size.

ideas.title: Apply RAG to broader language generation tasks
ideas.description: Extend RAG evaluation beyond QA to diverse knowledge-intensive generation tasks like dialogue or summarization.
ideas.sourceTitle: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

ideas.title: Dynamic Contextual Salience Prediction for Adaptive Attention
ideas.description: This approach trains a model with a dual objective: performing a primary task (e.g., QA, summarization) while simultaneously learning to predict which contiguous segment of the input context is most salient for that task. A lightweight auxiliary network scores context windows, and the model's attention mechanism is softly encouraged to prioritize the highest-scored region. This creates a content-driven, adaptive prioritization that can handle diverse information distributions without a rigid positional prior, improving robustness and task performance.

ideas.title: Integrate structured and unstructured knowledge
ideas.description: Combine Wikipedia text with infoboxes or knowledge graphs within the KILT framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Adaptive Hierarchical Sampling for Mid-Context Information Access
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution to the 'needle-in-a-haystack' problem in genomics—finding specific sequences within a massive genome—to the issue of mid-context performance degradation in long-input models. In genomics, hierarchical indexing and spaced sampling allow efficient location of target regions without exhaustive linear search. The adapted architectural approach involves a two-stage process: a lightweight, initial indexing network scans the full context to predict and tag segments with high informational salience (like potential 'key information' locations). This sparse guide then dynamically routes computation, allowing a primary transformer or state-space module to apply deeper, focused attention or processing specifically to these candidate mid-context regions. This mitigates the uniform attention dilution problem by architecturally prioritizing resource allocation to the middle of the context based on a learned, content-aware sampling strategy, rather than relying on fixed positional encodings or uniform attention across all tokens.

ideas.title: Relax independence of mention and entity encoding
ideas.description: Explore architectures where the bi-encoder does not process mention and entity independently.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Iterative Retrieval-Verification-Generation with Reinforcement Grounding
ideas.description: This system introduces a three-stage, iterative loop that architecturally enforces verifiability to prevent hallucination. First, a dedicated retriever proposes candidate passages from a corpus. Second, a separate verifier module scores each candidate for relevance to the query, providing a hard attention mask. Finally, a generator produces the answer conditioned solely on the top-verified evidence. The retriever is then improved via reinforcement learning, using the verifier's relevance scores as a reward signal, ensuring it learns to find better evidence without corrupting its core retrieval function.

ideas.title: Use contrastive learning for bi-encoder training
ideas.description: Incorporate contrastive loss functions from self-supervised learning to improve entity embeddings.
ideas.sourceTitle: Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Dynamic Ambiguous N-gram Indexing for Streaming Corpora
ideas.description: This research fuses dynamic indexing with ambiguous n-gram retrieval to create a system for streaming corpora where documents are continuously added or removed. Instead of maintaining unique identifiers for each n-gram, the index maps shared, ambiguous n-grams to multiple potential documents, drastically reducing update costs. The core innovation is a scoring model that performs real-time disambiguation by leveraging the evolving statistical context of the live corpus, such as co-occurrence patterns from recent updates. This allows the retrieval system to remain accurate and efficient even as the underlying meaning and prevalence of shared n-grams change over time.

ideas.title: Distill Retriever Scoring via Computational Fluid Dynamics Surrogate Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of surrogate modeling from computational fluid dynamics to the problem of identifier scoring in retrieval. In CFD, a precise but slow numerical solver is used to generate training data for a fast, approximate surrogate model that captures essential physical relationships. Analogously, the large, accurate retriever model is treated as the high-fidelity solver. Its scoring behavior is probed across a wide design space of queries, contexts, and candidate n-grams to create a dense dataset mapping inputs to scores. This dataset is then used to train a small, fast autoregressive model not just on final scores, but to emulate the retriever's underlying scoring function as a conditional generative process. The resulting distilled model can perform rapid, on-the-fly n-gram generation and scoring, preserving the retriever's quality while being deployable in latency-sensitive environments.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Interpretability for End-to-End Retrieval-Augmented Generation
ideas.description: This research direction builds an end-to-end model that jointly performs retrieval and generation, but crucially applies mechanistic interpretability tools to its internal retrieval mechanism. The goal is to directly observe why and how the model's integrated 'search' fails to access relevant information, especially from the middle of long contexts, during its forward pass. This internal, causal analysis will then inform targeted architectural interventions—such as modifying attention patterns or gradient pathways—specifically designed to fix the identified mechanistic flaws. The result is a co-design loop where interpretability guides the development of more robust and reliable end-to-end retrieval models.

ideas.title: Two-Stage Approximate N-gram Retrieval via LSH Filtering and Bounded Edit-Distance Verification
ideas.description: This redesigned system first uses a locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) scheme, like MinHash for Jaccard similarity, to map input n-grams and vocabulary n-grams to signature buckets, drastically reducing the candidate set for detailed comparison. In the second stage, it performs a precise, bounded edit-distance calculation only on the hashed candidates, ensuring that computational cost scales with the bucket size rather than the full vocabulary. This approach allows for tunable precision-recall trade-offs via LSH parameters and edit-distance thresholds, making it scalable for large-scale real-time applications. The result is a robust, efficient method for tolerant n-gram matching that avoids the intractable search space of the naive approach.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Dynamic Narrative Comprehension and Reasoning in Long Contexts
ideas.description: This research develops a benchmark framework that evaluates long-context models on their ability to perform dynamic reasoning and maintain narrative coherence, not just retrieve isolated facts. It uses procedural generation to create long, structured narratives with interleaved plots, shifting perspectives, and causal dependencies. The evaluation protocol presents sequences of questions that require tracking entities and events, resolving ambiguities, and making inferences based on information distributed across the entire context. This approach directly tests the functional utility of long-context understanding for complex tasks like dialogue, analysis, and multi-step problem-solving.

ideas.title: Psychometric Sequence Modeling for Adaptive Variable-Length Tokenization
ideas.description: This research applies the psychometric modeling of serial position curves—which capture the enhanced memorability of items at the beginning (primacy) and end (recency) of a sequence—to the problem of identifying meaningful substrings in text. Instead of using fixed-length n-grams, the system learns to segment text into variable-length phrases by predicting which contiguous character sequences will exhibit high 'cognitive salience' as measured by a U-shaped performance curve in downstream language model tasks. This allows the tokenizer to adaptively choose chunk sizes that maximize distinctiveness and predictive utility based on the structural context, fundamentally bridging psycholinguistic theory with efficient representation learning. The resulting tokenization strategy is both data-driven and cognitively grounded, potentially improving model efficiency and interpretability.

ideas.title: Invert the LM-as-KB comparison
ideas.description: Use structured KBs to improve language models, not just compare them.
ideas.sourceTitle: Language Models as Knowledge Bases?

ideas.title: Invert the retrieval-LM dependency
ideas.description: Use the LM's internal knowledge to guide or train the retrieval system, not vice-versa.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.title: Use memory networks for context integration
ideas.description: Incorporate ideas from memory-augmented neural networks to manage and reason over retrieved contexts.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.title: Active Contextual Beam Search for Structured Generation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes integrating the active querying of context with the structured exploration of beam search. Instead of treating the full context as a fixed input, the generation model can issue targeted queries to retrieve or highlight the most relevant contextual information at each step of the beam search. This allows the search algorithm to dynamically prune or prioritize hypotheses based on the actively structured context, significantly improving both efficiency and relevance. The result is a closed-loop system where beam search guides context interrogation, and the refined context, in turn, guides the search, leading to more coherent and contextually precise n-gram or sequence generation.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate query from entity
ideas.description: Study the dual task of generating context queries that would retrieve a given entity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Autoregressive Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Adaptive Variable-Length Phrase Tokens via Information-Theoretic Segmentation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that identifiers in language models must be fixed-length n-grams. Drawing an analogy from computational genomics, where tools like BLAST use variable-length 'words' or seeds adapted to the local sequence uniqueness for efficient and accurate alignment, we propose a similar adaptive segmentation for text. The solution is to dynamically determine substring length based on real-time calculation of information-theoretic measures (e.g., entropy, PMI) relative to a background corpus. A substring grows until it meets a pre-defined threshold of distinctiveness, creating variable-length phrase tokens. This improves coverage by allowing semantically coherent multi-word units to become single tokens, while also enhancing distinctiveness by ensuring each identifier carries sufficient informational weight, potentially leading to more efficient and semantically grounded representations in models like token-free language models or improved sparse retrieval methods.

ideas.title: Dual: evaluate knowledge source quality
ideas.description: Use model performance to identify gaps or errors in the knowledge corpus itself.
ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Query-to-Document Hashing via Forward-Only Sequence Alignment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that document retrieval must be modeled as generating query continuations from a document's context (right-to-left). Drawing an analogy from computational biology, specifically query-anchored sequence alignment, we adapt the solution of starting from the query sequence and extending forward to find matches. The adapted research idea is to generate document identifiers from the query left-to-right. As the query is processed token-by-token, it activates a dynamic set of candidate document IDs via a forward hash function that maps query n-gram prefixes to documents containing them. This creates a direct, streaming retrieval mechanism where the query 'aligns' to documents in a single forward pass, potentially improving efficiency and latency for real-time search systems.

ideas.title: Use information retrieval's term-based models
ideas.description: Hybridize dense vector retrieval with classic sparse, term-matching retrieval methods.
ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Robust Cross-Modal Retrieval via Fuzzy Substring Mapping
ideas.description: This research adapts the substring generation and mapping approach to multimodal retrieval by using descriptive text identifiers (like captions or attribute sets) as proxy keys for non-textual data (images, audio, video). Crucially, it integrates fuzzy string matching at the core of the mapping structure, allowing retrieval to tolerate typos, morphological variations, and paraphrases in the query text. This synergy enables a single, efficient system to perform robust similarity search across modalities without requiring perfect query formulation or expensive cross-modal embedding models for initial indexing. The result is a practical and scalable method for real-world applications where user-generated text queries are often imperfect or informal.

ideas.title: Inverse: generate contexts to highlight positions
ideas.description: Automatically create documents where key information is optimally positioned for model retrieval.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts

ideas.title: Scale down: probe smaller, efficient LMs
ideas.description: Investigate the knowledge-fidelity trade-off in smaller, distilled language models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Language Models as Knowledge Bases?

ideas.title: Relax the unsupervised constraint
ideas.description: Inject minimal supervision into the retrieval-augmentation pipeline to optimize the integration mechanism.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Context Affects Language Models' Factual Predictions

ideas.title: Develop better prompts for knowledge extraction
ideas.description: Create more effective cloze-style prompts to more accurately probe factual knowledge in LMs.

ideas.title: Dual: use LMs to generate KB training data
ideas.description: Use high-confidence LM predictions to automatically create or augment knowledge bases.

ideas.title: Scale down for domain-specific, small entity sets
ideas.description: Optimize the approach for high accuracy in specialized domains with limited, curated entities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scalable Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Mechanistic Interpretability of Disambiguation Failures in Long-Context Retrieval
ideas.description: This research direction uses ambiguous ngrams—phrases with multiple possible referents in a long document—as a diagnostic tool for long-context reasoning failures. By constructing retrieval tasks where the correct answer depends on disambiguating a shared ngram using mid-context clues, we can apply mechanistic interpretability to trace why models fail to access or integrate the necessary contextual information. This allows us to isolate whether failures stem from attention head saturation, poor key-value memory formation for middle tokens, or flawed compositional reasoning in later layers. The synergy lies in using the structured ambiguity of Idea B to create a precise, interpretable experimental framework for Idea A, moving from observing that models fail to understanding the specific circuit-level breakdowns that cause disambiguation failures in long contexts.

ideas.title: Apply program synthesis for structured tasks
ideas.description: Generate executable programs for slot filling from natural language queries.
ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Extend analysis to more tasks and models
ideas.description: Apply similar positional analysis to other long-context tasks like summarization or code generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts

ideas.title: Use a Masked Language Modeling approach for parallel identifier generation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that identifier generation must be sequential. Drawing an analogy from computational genomics, where models predict all amino acids in a protein fold in parallel (e.g., via masked language modeling on multiple sequence alignments), we adapt this to code. The solution is to replace the autoregressive LM with an encoder-only model trained with a masked identifier objective. Given a code snippet where identifier names are masked, the model uses the full bidirectional context of the code to predict all masked identifier tokens in a single, parallel forward pass. This could drastically speed up batch generation of candidate identifiers and allow token predictions to be conditioned on the entire future code context, not just the past.

ideas.title: Investigating Training Data Effects on Context Usage via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'common garden' experimental paradigm from ecology to investigate how training on long documents influences a model's ability to use information from different positions. In ecology, comparing organisms from different environments grown in a common garden isolates the effect of evolutionary history on traits. Analogously, we propose training multiple model instances from identical architectures but on meticulously controlled data corpora that differ only in key variables like average document length, positional bias of salient information (e.g., always at the beginning, middle, or end), and context span. These model 'lineages' are then evaluated on a shared test suite probing context usage (e.g., retrieval from specific positions, summarization of long texts, answering questions requiring information integration across a context). This method aims to causally disentangle the effects of training data structure from architectural biases on the emergent capability of using long-context information.

ideas.title: Scale to web-scale entity catalogs
ideas.description: Optimize the dense retrieval index for billions of entities with approximate nearest neighbor search.
ideas.sourceTitle: Zero-shot Entity Linking with Dense Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Transfer self-supervised learning from NLP
ideas.description: Adapt masked node prediction or contrastive learning from language models to pre-train on CFGs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Investigating Graph Embedding Neural Networks with Unsupervised Features Extraction for Binary Analysis

ideas.title: Locality-Sensitive Hashing of Context for Disambiguated, Direct Ngram Retrieval
ideas.description: This system pre-processes documents by generating a compact, locality-sensitive hash signature for the immediate context window surrounding each ngram. The ngram and this context signature are concatenated and hashed to form a unique, composite identifier that is directly indexable. This allows retrieval to use standard, efficient inverted index lookups on these composite keys, eliminating runtime disambiguation. The approach maintains the original goal of using shared lexical forms while offloading the disambiguation cost to an offline, parallelizable preprocessing stage.

ideas.title: Improve provenance and attribution capabilities
ideas.description: Enhance models to better cite their knowledge sources for fact verification.
ideas.sourceTitle: KILT: a Benchmark for Knowledge Intensive Language Tasks

ideas.title: Benchmarking and Isolating Integration Failures in Knowledge Synthesis
ideas.description: This research program posits that a primary failure mode in complex reasoning is the integration of information, not its retrieval. To test this concretely, we design a controlled benchmark where all relevant knowledge fragments are provided in-context, eliminating retrieval difficulty. The benchmark tasks require synthesizing these fragments—which may be contradictory, complementary, or hierarchical—into a single coherent answer or plan. Performance on this benchmark directly measures a model's integration capability, providing a falsifiable foundation for developing and evaluating new integration-focused architectures.

ideas.title: Create a dual: generate queries from documents

ideas.title: Contrastive Adversarial Distillation for Robust Identifier Scoring
ideas.description: This approach distills a large retriever's scoring function into a small autoregressive model using a two-objective training regime. Alongside standard distillation loss, a contrastive loss is applied where the student is trained to deviate from the teacher's score on carefully constructed adversarial n-gram examples. These adversarial examples are generated by an auxiliary model that perturbs high-confidence identifiers to create semantically plausible but likely incorrect variants. The resulting distilled model is faster for n-gram generation and exhibits improved robustness and calibration, especially on out-of-distribution data where the teacher model may be unreliable.

ideas.title: Scale number of tasks with shared biases

ideas.title: Compressed Suffix Arrays for High-Resolution Analysis of the 'Lost in the Middle' Effect
ideas.description: This research fuses the search for the minimal context length of the 'lost in the middle' effect with suffix array compression techniques to create a novel analysis framework. By representing the vast combinatorial space of possible short-context sequences and model attention patterns in a highly compressed, queryable data structure, we can systematically map the precise boundary where the effect emerges. This allows for efficient substring and pattern searches across millions of simulated attention outputs to identify specific token arrangements or syntactic structures that reliably induce the failure. The synergy provides both the high-resolution diagnostic capability of Idea A and the computational feasibility of Idea B, enabling a data-driven discovery of the minimal causal precursors to the phenomenon.

ideas.title: Scale task complexity to multi-document reasoning

ideas.title: Improve relevance scoring beyond NSP

ideas.title: Iterative Non-Autoregressive Sketching with Constrained Autoregressive Correction for Identifier Generation
ideas.description: This approach uses a two-stage model to overcome the coherence limitations of pure non-autoregressive generation. First, an encoder-only model produces an unordered set of likely candidate tokens and a rough length estimate in parallel (the 'sketch'). Second, a small, fast autoregressive decoder performs a constrained sequential pass only over the sketched tokens to assemble them into a grammatically valid and context-appropriate identifier, discarding invalid sequences. This hybrid design maintains significant parallelism for speed while guaranteeing the output quality and structural validity that pure NAR models lack.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Model Performance Across Task Topographies via a Standardized Test Battery
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cognitive psychology's use of standardized test batteries to map cognitive functions, this research proposes a systematic expansion of positional analysis. The analogous solution involves administering a controlled set of tasks (like a neuropsychological battery) to profile performance across different cognitive domains. Adapted to this context, the idea is to construct a standardized benchmark suite of diverse long-context tasks—such as retrieval, summarization, QA, and code generation—each designed to probe how model performance varies with input position. By applying identical positional analysis metrics across this battery for a range of models, the research will generate comparable, multi-dimensional performance profiles. This will allow for the identification of whether positional biases are consistent across the 'task topography' or if they manifest uniquely in certain task types or model architectures, providing a more general and foundational understanding of long-context processing limitations.

ideas.title: Hierarchical generation for long entity names

ideas.sourceTitle: Autoregressive Entity Retrieval

ideas.title: Use non-autoregressive models for identifier generation
ideas.description: Replace the autoregressive LM with a non-autoregressive or encoder-only model to generate all candidate identifiers in parallel.
ideas.sourceTitle: Autoregressive Search Engines: Generating Substrings as Document Identifiers

ideas.title: The Contextual Toxicity Problem: When Additional Information Impairs Inference
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that longer context windows always improve language model performance by drawing an analogy to hormesis in toxicology/ecology, where a substance is beneficial at low doses but harmful at higher ones. The analogous solution involves mapping the full non-monotonic dose-response curve to identify the optimal and toxic ranges. Adapted to NLP, the research would systematically test the 'context-response' relationship for specific tasks and positional distributions of key information (e.g., facts placed at the very beginning and end of a long document). The goal is to empirically map performance curves to identify if, when, and why additional context becomes 'toxic'—impairing inference rather than aiding it—thereby refining our understanding of context window utilization.

ideas.title: Integrate better provenance and knowledge updating
ideas.description: Enhance RAG to provide clear attribution and allow efficient updates to the non-parametric memory.

ideas.title: Apply program synthesis for knowledge manipulation

ideas.title: Graph-Sparsified On-Device Retrieval for Long-Context Mobile Agents
ideas.description: This research fuses ultra-lightweight on-device retrieval with graph-theoretic attention sparsification to create a new architecture for mobile agents. The core innovation is constructing a single, highly compressed graph index from the user's personal data (e.g., chat history, documents), where graph sparsification algorithms prune both the searchable knowledge links and, in parallel, define the sparse attention pathways for a small on-device language model. This dual-purpose structure allows the agent to retrieve relevant context and then perform inference over it using the same sparse connectivity pattern, drastically reducing memory and compute. The result is a practical mobile agent capable of maintaining and reasoning over long-context personal histories without cloud dependency, a capability neither compression nor sparsification alone could achieve.

ideas.title: Challenge the need for unique ngram identifiers

ideas.title: Use database indexing for context chunks

ideas.title: Dynamic Input-Conditioned Spectral Sparsification for Adaptive Attention
ideas.description: This approach introduces a learned, dynamic mechanism for attention sparsification that adapts to each input sequence. A small auxiliary network generates parameters for a stochastic sparsification process, which samples a sparse attention graph designed to preserve the spectral properties (and thus the informational structure) of the full attention graph for that specific context. The primary transformer layer then executes only this sparse, adaptive set of attention connections. This maintains the efficiency benefits of sparsity while restoring the model's capacity for input-dependent relational reasoning, overcoming the rigidity of static pruning methods.

ideas.title: Improve LMs' recall of less readily learned facts
